questions.text: Which of the following cell organelles is responsible for extracting energy from carbohydrates to form ATP?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Ribosomes are responsible for protein synthesis.
Chloroplast in plast contain pigments which trap sunlight energy and convert it to chemical energy (food).

Mitochondria are miniature biochemical factories where food stuffs or respiratory substrates are completely oxidised to carbon dioxide and water. The energy liberated in the process is initially stored in the form of reduced coenzymes and reduced prosthetic groups.The latter soon undergo oxidation and form energy rich ATP. ATP comes out of mitochondria and helps perform various energy requiring processes of the cell like muscle contraction, nerve impulse conduction, biosynthesis, membrane transport, cell division, movement, etc. Because of the formation of ATP, the mitochondria are called power houses of the cell..
Lysosomes are responsible for intracellular digestion.

questions.explanation: Mitochondria are miniature biochemical factories where food stuffs or respiratory substrates are completely oxidised to carbon dioxide and water. ATP comes out of mitochondria and helps perform various energy requiring processes of the cell like muscle contraction, nerve impulse conduction, biosynthesis, membrane transport, cell division, movement, etc. Because of the formation of ATP, the mitochondria are called power houses of the cell. A.Ribosomes are responsible for protein synthesis. B.Chloroplast in plast contain pigments which trap sunlight energy and convert it to chemical energy (food). D.Lysosomes are responsible for intracellular digestion.
hints: extracting energy from carbohydrates to form ATP

questions.text: A cell organelle containing hydrolytic enzymes is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Lysosomes are small vesicles which are bounded by a single membrane and contain hydrolytic enzymes. About 50 enzymes have been recorded to occur in them. The important enzymes are acid phosphatases, sulphatases, proteases, peptidases, nucleases, lipases, and carbohydrases. They are also called acid hydrolases because these digestive enzymes usually function in acidic medium of pH of 4­-5 and help in intracellular digestion.
Microbodies include peroxisomes and glyoxysomes. Peroxisomes are responsible for detoxification of alcohols and contain oxidative enzymes. Glyoxysomes contain enzymes for plant lipid metabolism.
Ribosomes are responsible for protein synthesis. They donot contain hydrolytic enzymes.
These are invagination of plasma membrane in prokaryotes. These help in DNA replication and respiration.

Nucleosomes do not contain centrioles.

Nucleus is a double membrane bound structure.

questions.text: Which of the following structures is not found in a prokaryotic cell?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mesosomes are invagination of plasma membrane found in prokaryotes.
Prokaryotes have plasma membrane and cell wall as well.

A prokaryotic cell is characterised by absence of an organised nucleus and membrane bound cell organelles. DNA is naked i.e., without a nuclear envelope and coiled in the cytoplasm. It is commonly called nucleoid or genophore.
Prokaryotes have 70S ribosomes found in matrix of mitochondria and stroma of chloroplast.

questions.text: The nuclear envelope is a derivative of:

options.explanation: Microtubules form spindle fibres in plant cell and in animals it form cilia, flagella, centrioles and basal body.

The nuclear envelope transmembrane proteins are absorbed in the RER. Once the division is completed, RER reassembles the nuclear envelope.
Smooth endoplasmic reticulum are involved in metabolism of carbohydrates, synthesis of lipids and transport of cellular products.
Golgi complex is involved in processing of cell secretions.

questions.text: The structures that are formed by stacking of organised flattened membranous sacs in the chloroplasts are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cisternae are stack of flattened, membrane bound sacs forming Golgi complex.
Stroma is the fluid matrix of chloroplast.
Cristae are foldings of inner membrane of mitochondria.

A chloroplast is a vesicle,bound by an envelope of two unit membranes and filled with a fluid matrix called stroma. Inside the stroma are double membranous, flattened sacs called thylakoids. Thylakoids are stacked or piled up forming grana.

questions.explanation: A chloroplast is a vesicle, bound by an envelope of two unit membranes and filled with a fluid matrix called stroma. A.Cisternae are stack of flattened, membrane bound sacs forming Golgi complex. B.Stroma is the fluid matrix of chloroplast. C.Cristae are foldings of inner membrane of mitochondria.

questions.text: The solid linear cytoskeletal elements having a diameter of 6 nm and made up of a single type of monomer are known as
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Microtubules are hollow microscopic tubular structures with an external diameter of 24 nm and of variable length.
Microfilaments are fine, thread-like protein fibers, 3-6 nm in diameter. They are composed predominantly of a contractile protein called actin.

Intermediate filaments are the numerous microscopic protein fibers of about 10 nm thickness that form part of the cytoskeleton.They are intermediate in size between microtubules and microfilaments.They are made up of a variety of proteins e.g. keratin in nails.
Cilia are formed of microtubules which are 24 nm in diameter.

Plastids are responsible for photosynthesis and food storage.
Ribosomes are involved in protein synthesis.

questions.text: The Golgi complex plays a major role
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Golgi complex does not transfer energy.

options.text: in post translational modification of proteins and glycosylation of lipids
options.explanation: Post translational modification (PTM) is a step in protein biosynthesis. Proteins are synthesized in ribosomes translating mRNA into polypeptide chains. These polypeptide chains undergo PTM, such as folding, cutting, and other processes, before becoming the mature protein product. Proteins synthesized by the rough endoplasmic reticulum and lipids synthesized by smooth endoplasmic reticulum reach the cisternae of the Golgi apparatus. Here, they combine with carbohydrates to form glycoproteins and glycolipids. This process is called glycosylation.

options.text: in trapping the light and transforming it nto chemical energy
options.explanation: This is the role of chlorophyll present in chloroplast.
Intracellular digestion is the role of lysosomes.

questions.text: The term ‘glycocalyx’ is used for:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: A layer present between cell wall and membrane of bacteria
options.explanation: The periplasm is the space that exists between the cell wall and the plasma membrane in gram negative bacteria.
cell wall of bacteria is composed of peptidoglycan and is not the glycocalyx.

options.text: Bacterial cell glyco­engineered to possess N-­glycosylated proteins
options.explanation: It does not have any connection with glycocalyx.

options.text: A layer surrounding the cell wall of bacteria.
options.explanation: 'Glycocalyx' is a sticky, gelatinous material that collects outside the cell wall of bacteria to form an additional surface layer. When this layer is firmly attached to the surface of the cell, it is called a 'capsule'. If it is loosely distributed around the cell, the glycocalyx is called a 'slime layer'.

questions.text: Select the correct statement from the following regarding the cell membrane.
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Na+ and K+ ions move across cell membrane by passive transport.
options.explanation: The movement of Na+ and K+ ions across the cell membrane does not occur solely by passive transport. It primarily occurs through an active transport process called the sodium-potassium pump or Na+/K+-ATPase.

options.text: Proteins make up 60 to 70% of the cell membrane.
options.explanation: Proteins make up 60 to 80% of the cell membrane.

options.text: Lipids are arranged in a bilayer with polar heads towards the inner part.
options.explanation: Polar heads are the outer hydrophillic parts; whereas the tails are hydrophobic and non-polar.

options.text: Fluid mosaic model of cell membrane was proposed by Singer and Nicolson.
options.explanation: According to the fluid mosaic model of cell membrane structure proposed by Singer and Nicolson (1972), the plasma membrane contains about 60–80% proteins and 20–40% lipids. Lipids form a bilayer with hydrophilic heads pointing outwards. The cell membrane allows transport of some molecules by passive transport e.g., water, neutral solutes while some are transported actively e.g., Na+ /K + pump.

questions.explanation: According to the fluid mosaic model of cell membrane structure proposed by Singer and Nicolson (1972), the plasma membrane contains about 60–80% proteins and 20–40% lipids. Lipids form a bilayer with hydrophilic heads pointing outwards. The cell membrane allows transport of some molecules by passive transport e.g., water, neutral solutes while some are transported actively e.g., Na+ /K + pump.

questions.text: What is true about ribosomes?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The prokaryotic ribosomes are 80S, where “S” stands for sedimentation coefficient.
options.explanation: The prokaryotic ribosomes are 70S; whereas the eukaryotic ribosomes are 80S.

options.text: These are composed of ribonucleic acid and proteins.
options.explanation: A ribosome is a small, spherical body within a living cell that is the site of protein synthesis. Ribosomes consist of two subunits, one large and one small, each of which comprises some RNA (called ribosomal RNA) and protein. They do not have any DNA.

options.text: These are found only in eukaryotic cells.
options.explanation: Ribosomes are found in both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells.

options.text: These are self ­splicing introns of some RNAs.
options.explanation: They are not self-splicing introns. Self-splicing introns are non-coding regions within genes that can remove themselves from RNA molecules during a process called self-splicing. Ribosomes, on the other hand, are responsible for translating mRNA into proteins and are not involved in self-splicing intron removal.

questions.explanation: A ribosome is a small, spherical body within a living cell that is the site of protein synthesis. Ribosomes consist of two subunits, one large and one small, each of which comprises some RNA (called ribosomal RNA) and protein. They do not have any DNA.

questions.subject: Biology

E.coli (bacteria) is a prokaryote while Chlamydomonas (algae) is a eukaryote. Cell wall organization differs as bacterial cell wall is rich in muramic acid while algal cell wall is cellulosic.
E.coli (bacteria) is a prokaryote while Chlamydomonas (algae) is a eukaryote. The cell membrane has similar organization in both the groups.

questions.text: Which of the following cellular parts is correctly described?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Thylakoids - ­flattened membranous sacs forming the grana of chloroplasts
options.explanation: Thylakoids are the flattened sac-­like membranous structures that are stacked on top of one another to form the grana of plant chloroplast. Chlorophyll and other photosynthetic pigments are situated in the thylakoid membranes, which are the site for the light­-dependent reactions of photosynthesis.

options.text: Centrioles ­- sites for active RNA synthesis
options.explanation: Centrioles play a crucial role in cell division, organizing and forming the spindle fibers that help separate chromosomes during mitosis and meiosis. however, The sites for active RNA synthesis in a cell are the nucleolus and the nucleus.

options.text: Ribosomes -­ those on chloroplasts are larger (80S) while those in the cytoplasm are smaller (70S)
options.explanation: Chloroplasts have 70S ribosomes where as eukaryotic cytoplasm have 80S ribosomes and prokaryotic cytoplasm have 70S ribosomes.

options.text: Lysosomes ­- optimally active at a pH of about 8.5
options.explanation: Lysosomes are optimally active at an acidic pH, typically around 4.5 to 5.0.

questions.explanation: Thylakoids are the flattened sac-­like membranous structures that are stacked on top of one another to form the grana of plant chloroplast. Chlorophyll and other photosynthetic pigments are situated in the thylakoid membranes, which are the site for the light­-dependent reactions of photosynthesis.

questions.text: Which one of the following structures is an organelle within an organelle?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Ribosomes are the location of protein synthesis in a cell is a structure, composed of both RNA and protein. They manufacture amino acids and are present inside other cell organelles like mitochondria, chloroplast and endoplasmic reticulum. Hence, they are referred to as organelle within an organelle.
In eukaryotic cells, there are lysosome-like organelles called Peroxisomes. Toxins such as hydrogen peroxide or metabolites are removed by Peroxisomes.
The endoplasmic reticulum is a vast network of membranous sheets and tubes.They are termed the cell's transport system because they are loaded with fluids and convey things throughout the cell.
Convoluted membranous structures formed by plasma membrane infoldings in the pattern of vesicles, tubules, or lamellar whorls are termed mesosomes.

questions.text: Peptide synthesis inside a cell takes place in the:

Mitochondria provides energy to the cell and is the power house of the cell.
Chromoplast is responsible for giving colours to coloured part of plants such as petals, fruits etc.

Peptide synthesis inside a cell takes place in the ribosome. Ribosomes are found in all cells and are involved in protein synthesis. The major constituents of ribosomes are RNA and proteins present in approximately equal amounts.

questions.text: An important site for formation of glycoproteins and glycolipids is
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The function of vacuoles is to store substances such as water, nutrients, pigments, and toxins, maintain turgor pressure in plant cells, dispose of waste materials, detoxify harmful compounds, and store pigments for coloration.

Eukaryotic cells contain a unique cluster of membrane vesicles known as the Golgi apparatus. It principally performs the function of packaging materials. The newly synthesized proteins are handed over to the Golgi apparatus which is causes the addition of carbohydrates, lipid or sulphates moieties to the proteins. Golgi apparatus is an important site for the formation of glycoproteins and glycolipids.

The function of plastids is to perform various roles, including photosynthesis, storage of pigments, synthesis and storage of starch, lipid metabolism, and other specialized functions depending on the type of plastid.
The function of lysosomes is to digest and break down waste materials, cellular debris, and foreign substances through the action of hydrolytic enzymes.

questions.text: Which one of the following is not considered as a part of the endomembrane system?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While each of the membranous organelles is distinct in terms of its structure and function, many of these are considered together as an endomembrane system because their functions are coordinated. The endomembrane system include endoplasmic reticulum (ER), Golgi complex, lysosomes and vacuoles.

While each of the membranous organelles is distinct in terms of its structure and function, many of these are considered together as an endomembrane system because their functions are coordinated. The endomembrane system include endoplasmic reticulum (ER), Golgi complex, lysosomes and vacuoles. Since the functions of the mitochondria, chloroplast and peroxisomes are not coordinated with the above components, these are not considered as part of the endomembrane system.

While each of the membranous organelles is distinct in terms of its structure and function, many of these are considered together as an endomembrane system because their functions are coordinated. The endomembrane system include endoplasmic reticulum (ER), Golgi complex, lysosomes and vacuoles.

questions.explanation: While each of the membranous organelles is distinct in terms of its structure and function, many of these are considered together as an endomembrane system because their functions are coordinated. The endomembrane system include endoplasmic reticulum (ER), Golgi complex, lysosomes and vacuoles. Since the functions of the mitochondria, chloroplast and peroxisomes are not coordinated with the above components, these are not considered as part of the endomembrane system.

questions.text: The figure below shows the structure of a mitochondrion with its four parts labelled A, B, C and D. Select the part correctly matched with its function.
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: D (outer membrane) – gives rise to inner membrane by splitting
options.explanation: The function of the mitochondrial outer membrane is to serve as a protective barrier and regulate the passage of molecules in and out of the mitochondria.

options.text: B (inner membrane) – forms infoldings called cristae
options.explanation: The inner membrane gives rise to several infoldings called cristae. The function of cristae is to increase the surface area of the inner mitochondrial membrane, allowing for more efficient production of ATP through cellular respiration.

options.text: C (crista) – possesses single circular DNA molecule and ribosomes
options.explanation: Mitochondrial cristae are folds of the mitochondrial inner membrane that provide an increase in the surface area. This allows a greater space for processes that happen across this membrane such as ATP synthesis.

options.text: A (matrix) – major site for respiratory chain enzymes
options.explanation: The respiratory chain enzymes are located in the inner mitochondrial membrane.

questions.explanation: Each mitochondrion is a double membrane­ bound structure with the outer membrane and the inner membrane dividing its lumen distinctly into two aqueous compartments, i.e., the outer compartment and the inner compartment. The inner compartment is called the matrix. The outer membrane forms the continuous limiting boundary of the organelle. The inner membrane forms a number of infoldings called the cristae towards the matrix. The cristae increase the surface area. The two membranes have their own specific enzymes associated with the mitochondrial function.
hints: part correctly matched with its function

questions.text: The plasma membrane consists mainly of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Phospholipids embedded in a protein bilayer
options.explanation: Proteins are embedded in a phospholipid bilayer.

options.text: Proteins embedded in a phospholipid bilayer
options.explanation: Plasma membrane consists of lipids (20-­40%), proteins (60­-80%), carbohydrates (1-­5%) and water . Lipid molecules possess both hydrophobic and hydrophilic ends and are thus arranged in the form of lipid bilayer. The most common lipids of the bilayer are phospholipids. Protein molecules occur at places both inside (intrinsic proteins) and on the outer side (extrinsic proteins) of the phospholipid bilayer.

options.text: Proteins embedded in a polymer of glucose molecules
options.explanation: Proteins are embedded in a phospholipid bilayer and not in polymer of glucose molecules.

options.text: Proteins embedded in a carbohydrate bilayer.
options.explanation: Proteins are embedded in a phospholipid bilayer and not in charbohydrate bilayer.

questions.explanation: Plasma membrane consists of lipids (20-­40%), proteins (60­-80%), carbohydrates (1-­5%) and water . Lipid molecules possess both hydrophobic and hydrophilic ends and are thus arranged in the form of lipid bilayer. The most common lipids of the bilayer are phospholipids. Protein molecules occur at places both inside (intrinsic proteins) and on the outer side (extrinsic proteins) of the phospholipid bilayer.

questions.text: The main area of various types of activities in a cell is the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The function of the plasma membrane is to regulate the movement of substances in and out of the cell, maintain cell integrity, and facilitate cell communication.
The function of mitochondria is to generate energy in the form of ATP through cellular respiration.

Cytoplasm is a granular, crystallo-­colloidal complex that forms the living protoplasm of a cell excluding its nucleus. It consists of proteins, nucleic acids, fats, carbohydrates, vitamins, minerals, waste metabolites, and all the organelles. It is the main area for various types of activities of a cell like respiration, nutrition, storage, etc.
The function of the nucleus is to store genetic material (DNA), control cellular activities, and regulate gene expression.

questions.explanation: Cytoplasm is a granular, crystallo-­colloidal complex that forms the living protoplasm of a cell excluding its nucleus. It consists of proteins, nucleic acids, fats, carbohydrates, vitamins, minerals, waste metabolites, and all the organelles. It is the main area for various types of activities of a cell like respiration, nutrition, storage, etc.
hints: main area of various types of activities

questions.text: Which one of the following has its own DNA?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mitochondria have their own DNA. They are double-membraned organelles found in eukaryotic cells responsible for producing energy through cellular respiration.
Incorrect. Dictyosomes (also known as the Golgi apparatus) are involved in modifying, sorting, and packaging proteins. They do not have their own DNA.
Incorrect. Lysosomes are responsible for intracellular digestion and waste removal. They do not have their own DNA.
Incorrect. Peroxisomes are involved in breaking down fatty acids and detoxifying harmful substances. They do not have their own DNA.

questions.explanation: Mitochondria have their own DNA. They are double-membraned organelles found in eukaryotic cells responsible for producing energy through cellular respiration.

questions.text: Which one of the following structures between two adjacent plant cells is an effective transport pathway?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Plasmodesmata are microscopic channels that connect the cytoplasm of adjacent plant cells. They allow for the direct transport of various substances, such as ions, nutrients, and signaling molecules, between the cells. Plasmodesmata are an effective transport pathway for facilitating communication and coordination between plant cells.

Plastoquinones are electron carriers in the photosynthetic electron transport chain of chloroplasts. They are not involved in transport between adjacent plant cells; instead, they participate in the transport of electrons during the process of photosynthesis.

The endoplasmic reticulum (ER) is an organelle involved in protein synthesis, folding, and lipid metabolism within cells. While it plays essential roles in intracellular transport, it is not a direct transport pathway between adjacent plant cells.

Plasmalemma is another term for the plasma membrane, which surrounds the cell and regulates the movement of substances in and out of the cell. While it controls the exchange of materials into and out of individual plant cells, it is not a specialized structure for transport between adjacent plant cells.

questions.explanation: Plasmodesmata are microscopic channels that connect the cytoplasm of adjacent plant cells. They allow for the direct transport of various substances, such as ions, nutrients, and signaling molecules, between the cells. Plasmodesmata are an effective transport pathway for facilitating communication and coordination between plant cells.

questions.text: The middle lamella is composed mainly of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Muramic acid is a component of bacterial cell walls, not present in the middle lamella of plant cells.

The middle lamella is composed mainly of calcium pectate. Calcium pectate is a complex polysaccharide that acts as a cementing substance between adjacent plant cell walls, holding them together. It is rich in pectin, a type of carbohydrate found in plant cell walls.
Phosphoglycerides are a type of lipid that makes up cell membranes, not a major component of the middle lamella.
Hemicellulose is a type of polysaccharide found in the primary cell wall of plant cells, but it is not the main component of the middle lamella.

questions.explanation: The middle lamella is composed mainly of calcium pectate. Calcium pectate is a complex polysaccharide that acts as a cementing substance between adjacent plant cell walls, holding them together. It is rich in pectin, a type of carbohydrate found in plant cell walls.

questions.text: Cytoskeleton is made up of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Callose is a type of polysaccharide, and while it is involved in various plant cell processes, it is not a major component of the cytoskeleton.
Cellulose is a polysaccharide that forms the primary component of plant cell walls but is not part of the cytoskeleton.

The cytoskeleton is made up of proteinaceous filaments, which are long, thread-like structures that provide structural support, maintain cell shape, and play a crucial role in cell movement and intracellular transport. These filaments include microtubules, microfilaments (actin filaments), and intermediate filaments.
Calcium carbonate is a chemical compound that can be found in certain organisms as mineral structures, such as shells and skeletons. However, it is not a component of the cytoskeleton.

questions.explanation: The cytoskeleton is made up of proteinaceous filaments, which are long, thread-like structures that provide structural support, maintain cell shape, and play a crucial role in cell movement and intracellular transport. These filaments include microtubules, microfilaments (actin filaments), and intermediate filaments.

questions.text: Plasmodesmata are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Plasmodesmata are not locomotory structures; they do not play a role in cell movement or locomotion.

options.text: Membranes connecting the nucleus with plasmalemma
options.explanation: Plasmodesmata connect the cytoplasm of adjacent cells, not the nucleus with the plasmalemma (cell membrane).

Plasmodesmata are microscopic channels that connect the cytoplasm of adjacent plant cells, allowing communication and transport of various substances between them. They are essential for cell-to-cell communication and are involved in the exchange of nutrients, signals, and other molecules between neighboring plant cells.
Plasmodesmata are not lignified or cemented layers; they are dynamic channels that allow direct communication between plant cells.

questions.explanation: Plasmodesmata are microscopic channels that connect the cytoplasm of adjacent plant cells, allowing communication and transport of various substances between them. They are essential for cell-to-cell communication and are involved in the exchange of nutrients, signals, and other molecules between neighboring plant cells.

questions.text: In germinating seeds, fatty acids are degraded exclusively in the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Peroxisomes are involved in various metabolic processes, including the breakdown of hydrogen peroxide and fatty acids. However, in germinating seeds, fatty acid degradation occurs in glyoxysomes, not in regular peroxisomes.

Mitochondria are responsible for energy production through aerobic respiration. While fatty acids can also be degraded in mitochondria through beta-oxidation, this process is not specific to germinating seeds.

Proplastids are early-stage plastids found in plant cells that can differentiate into other types of plastids (e.g., chloroplasts, chromoplasts). They are not directly involved in fatty acid degradation during seed germination.

In germinating seeds, fatty acids are degraded exclusively in glyoxysomes. Glyoxysomes are specialized peroxisomes found in plant cells that are involved in the conversion of stored fats (lipids) into carbohydrates during seed germination.

questions.explanation: In germinating seeds, fatty acids are degraded exclusively in glyoxysomes. Glyoxysomes are specialized peroxisomes found in plant cells that are involved in the conversion of stored fats (lipids) into carbohydrates during seed germination.

questions.text: Vacuole in a plant cell
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because vacuoles in plant cells are membrane-bound organelles and do not contain air.

options.text: lacks membrane and contains water and excretory substances
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because plant cell vacuoles are membrane-bound and do not contain excretory substances.

options.text: is membrane-­bound and contains storage proteins and lipids
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because plant cell vacuoles primarily store water, nutrients, and waste products, not storage proteins and lipids.

options.text: is membrane-­bound and contains water and excretory substances.
options.explanation: A vacuole in a plant cell is a membrane-bound organelle that functions as a storage compartment. It contains water, nutrients, ions, and waste products. The central vacuole in plant cells is primarily filled with water and serves various purposes, such as maintaining cell turgidity and storing essential substances.

questions.explanation: A vacuole in a plant cell is a membrane-bound organelle that functions as a storage compartment. It contains water, nutrients, ions, and waste products. The central vacuole in plant cells is primarily filled with water and serves various purposes, such as maintaining cell turgidity and storing essential substances.

questions.text: The two sub-­units of ribosome remain united at a critical ion level of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The correct option is A) magnesium. Ribosomes are composed of two subunits, a small subunit and a large subunit, which remain united with the help of magnesium ions. Magnesium ions play a crucial role in stabilizing the ribosome's structure and facilitating protein synthesis.

Calcium ions do not play a significant role in keeping the ribosome subunits united. Calcium ions have other important functions in the cell, but they are not directly involved in ribosome assembly or stability.
Copper ions are not involved in the union of ribosome subunits. Copper has essential roles in various enzymes, but it is not a critical ion for ribosome function.
Manganese ions are not critical for keeping the ribosome subunits united. While manganese is an important trace element, it is not directly involved in ribosomal function or structure.

questions.explanation: The correct option is A) magnesium. Ribosomes are composed of two subunits, a small subunit and a large subunit, which remain united with the help of magnesium ions. Magnesium ions play a crucial role in stabilizing the ribosome's structure and facilitating protein synthesis.

questions.text: Keeping in view the ‘fluid mosaic model' for the structure of cell membrane, which one of the following statements is correct with respect to the movements of lipids and proteins from one lipid monolayer to the other (described as flipflop movement)?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: While proteins can flip­-flop, lipids can not
options.explanation: Lipids can flip-flop, they do so slowly with the help of protein called Flippases. But protein do not flip-flop

options.text: Neither lipids, nor proteins can flip­-flop
options.explanation: Lipids can flip-flop, they do so slowly with the help of protein called Flippases. But protein do not flip-flop

options.text: Both lipids and proteins can flip-­flop
options.explanation: Lipids can flip-flop, they do so slowly with the help of protein called Flippases. But protein do not flip-flop

options.text: While lipids can rarely flip-­flop, proteins cannot
options.explanation: Lipids can flip-flop, they do so slowly with the help of protein called Flippases. But protein do not flip-flop

questions.explanation: Lipids can flip-flop, they do so slowly with the help of protein called Flippases. But protein do not flip-flop

questions.text: Which one of the following is not a constituent of the cell membrane?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Glycolipids are constituents of the cell membrane. They are lipids (fats) that have a carbohydrate chain attached to them. These molecules play essential roles in cell recognition, cell signaling, and maintaining the stability and fluidity of the cell membrane.

Proline is an amino acid and is not a constituent of the cell membrane. Cell membranes are primarily composed of phospholipids, cholesterol, and glycolipids. Phospholipids form the basic structural framework, cholesterol provides stability, and glycolipids contribute to cell recognition and communication. However, proline's primary role is in protein structure and function, not in the composition of the cell membrane.

Phospholipids are the major constituents of the cell membrane. They have a hydrophilic (water-attracting) head and two hydrophobic (water-repelling) tails. This unique structure allows phospholipids to form a bilayer in the cell membrane, with their hydrophilic heads facing outward and their hydrophobic tails facing inward, creating a selectively permeable barrier for the cell.

Cholesterol is also a constituent of the cell membrane. It is a type of lipid that is interspersed between the phospholipids in the cell membrane. Cholesterol helps regulate membrane fluidity and stability by preventing the fatty acid chains of phospholipids from packing too closely together or too far apart.

questions.explanation: Chemically, a biomembrane consists of lipids (20-­70%), proteins (20­-70%), carbohydrates (1-­5%) and water (20%). The important lipids of the membrane are phospholipids (some hundred types), sterols, (e.g. cholesterol), glycolipids and sphingolipidss (e.g., sphingomyelin, cerebrosides). Proteins can be enzymatic, fibrous or globular structural carriers or act as receptors.
hints: not a constituent of the cell membrane

questions.text: Select the wrong statement from the following.
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Both chloroplasts and mitochondria contain DNA.
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Both organelles have their own DNA, allowing them to carry out some functions independently within the cell.

options.text: The mitochondria are generally smaller than chloroplast
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Mitochondria are typically smaller than chloroplasts.

options.text: Both chloroplasts and mitochondria contain an inner and an outer membrane
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Both organelles have a double-membrane structure with an inner and an outer membrane.

options.text: Both chloroplasts and mitochondria have an internal compartment - the thylakoid space bounded by the thylakoid membrane
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. While chloroplasts have an internal compartment called the thylakoid space, mitochondria do not have a thylakoid space. The thylakoid space is where the light-dependent reactions of photosynthesis occur in chloroplasts.

questions.explanation: The statement D is incorrect. While chloroplasts have an internal compartment called the thylakoid space, mitochondria do not have a thylakoid space. The thylakoid space is where the light-dependent reactions of photosynthesis occur in chloroplasts.

questions.text: Biological organization starts at the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Biological organization starts at the cellular level because living organisms are composed of cells, which are the basic structural and functional units of life. Cells come together to form tissues, and tissues combine to form organs, ultimately leading to the entire organism's structure and function.

Organismic level refers to the entire individual organism as a whole, which is a higher level of biological organization. It is not the starting point of biological organization; rather, it is the result of the integration and coordination of various lower-level components, including cells, tissues, and organs.

The atomic level is even lower than the cellular level, dealing with individual atoms that make up molecules. While important in biochemical processes, it does not encompass the fundamental unit of life (cell) or the hierarchical organization of living organisms.

The submicroscopic molecular level deals with molecules, which are composed of atoms and involved in various biological processes. However, it is not the starting point of biological organization. Biological organization begins with cells, which are more complex structures than individual molecules.

questions.explanation: Biological organization starts at the cellular level because living organisms are composed of cells, which are the basic structural and functional units of life. Cells come together to form tissues, and tissues combine to form organs, ultimately leading to the entire organism's structure and function.

questions.text: Which of the following statements regarding mitochondrial membrane is not correct ?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The outer membrane resembles a sieve.
options.explanation: The outer membrane resembles a sieve due to presence of porins.

options.text: The outer membrane is permeable to all kinds of molecules.
options.explanation: The outer membrane of mitochondrion is smooth, freely permeable to all kinds of molecules.

options.text: The enzymes of the electron transfer chain are embedded in the outer membrane.
options.explanation: The enzymes of the electron transfer chain are not embedded in the outer membrane. They are present in inner membrane.

options.text: The inner membrane is highly convoluted forming a series of infoldings.
options.explanation: The inner membrane is usually produced into numerous infolds called cristae (singular crista).

questions.explanation: The outer membrane of mitochondrion is smooth, and freely permeable to all kinds of molecules. It has porin proteins which form channels for the passage of molecules through it. It allows the uptake of substrates and the release of ATP. The inner membrane is semipermeable and regulates the passage of materials into and out of the mitochondrion. It is rich in enzymes and carrier proteins (permeases). It is usually produced into numerous infolds called cristae (singular crista). It bears minute regularly spaced lollipop-­shaped particles known as oxysomes. The rest of the inner membrane contains the electron carrier molecules of the electron transport chain.

questions.text: Which of the following statements regarding cilia is NOT correct?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Cilia contain an outer ring of nine doublet microtubules surrounding two singlet microtubules.
options.explanation: Cilia contain an outer ring of nine doublet microtubules surrounding two singlet microtubules, forming a 9+2 arrangement.

options.text: The organized beating of cilia is controlled by fluxes of Ca2+ across the membrane.
options.explanation: Option B is incorrect because the organized beating of cilia is not controlled by fluxes of Ca2+ but by the sliding of microtubule doublets.
Cilia are hair-like cellular appendages.

options.text: Microtubules of cilia are composed of tubulin
options.explanation: Microtubules of cilia are composed of tubulin.

questions.explanation: Option B is incorrect because the organized beating of cilia is not controlled by fluxes of Ca2+ but by the sliding of microtubule doublets.

questions.text: A major breakthrough in the studies of cells came with the development of the electron microscope. This is because:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The electron microscope is more powerful than the light microscope as it uses a beam of electrons which has wavelength much longer than that of photons
options.explanation: Incorrect. The electron microscope uses a beam of electrons, which actually has a much shorter wavelength than photons used in light microscopes. Shorter wavelengths enable higher resolution, not longer wavelengths.

options.text: The resolving power of the electron microscope is much higher than that of the light microscope
options.explanation: The correct option is B because the major breakthrough with the development of the electron microscope lies in its significantly higher resolving power compared to the light microscope. The resolving power of a microscope determines its ability to distinguish fine details. Electron microscopes use a beam of electrons with much shorter wavelengths than photons, allowing them to resolve much smaller structures and achieve much higher magnifications. This advancement revolutionized cell biology, enabling scientists to study cellular structures and organelles at a level of detail that was not possible with light microscopes.

options.text: The resolving power of the electron microscope is 200-350 nm as compared to 0.1­-0.2 nm for the light microscope
options.explanation: Incorrect. The resolving power of the electron microscope is not 200-350 nm; rather, it can resolve structures down to the nanometer (nm) scale, typically in the range of 0.1-0.2 nm. Light microscopes, on the other hand, have a resolving power limited to around 200-350 nm.

options.text: The electron beam can pass through thick materials, whereas light microscopy requires thin sections
options.explanation: Incorrect. Both electron microscopes and light microscopes can pass through thick materials, but the key advantage of electron microscopes is their ability to achieve much higher resolution due to the shorter wavelength of electrons. Light microscopy may require thin sections for certain types of samples, but it can still image thick materials in some applications.

questions.explanation: The correct option is B because the major breakthrough with the development of the electron microscope lies in its significantly higher resolving power compared to the light microscope. The resolving power of a microscope determines its ability to distinguish fine details. Electron microscopes use a beam of electrons with much shorter wavelengths than photons, allowing them to resolve much smaller structures and achieve much higher magnifications. This advancement revolutionized cell biology, enabling scientists to study cellular structures and organelles at a level of detail that was not possible with light microscopes.
hints: breakthrough in the studies of cells

questions.text: Chlorophyll in chloroplasts is located in the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Chlorophyll in chloroplasts is located in the grana. Grana are stacks of thylakoid membranes inside the chloroplasts where the photosystems containing chlorophyll are embedded. They are responsible for capturing light energy during photosynthesis.

Pyrenoid is not found in chloroplasts but in the chloroplasts of certain algae, specifically in the chloroplast of certain unicellular algae called green algae. Pyrenoid is involved in the synthesis of starch.

Chlorophyll is not primarily located in the stroma. The stroma is the fluid-filled space surrounding the grana within the chloroplast, where various enzymes for the Calvin cycle (dark reactions) are present.

This option is incorrect. Chlorophyll is primarily located in the grana, not in the stroma. The grana contain the chlorophyll-rich photosystems responsible for capturing light energy during photosynthesis. The stroma contains other enzymes involved in the dark reactions of photosynthesis.

questions.explanation: Chlorophyll in chloroplasts is located in the grana. Grana are stacks of thylakoid membranes inside the chloroplasts where the photosystems containing chlorophyll are embedded. They are responsible for capturing light energy during photosynthesis.

questions.text: A student wishes to study the cell structure under a light microscope having 10X eyepiece and 45X objective. He should illuminate the object by which one of the following colours of light so as to get the best possible resolution?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The resolution of a light microscope is inversely proportional to the wavelength of light used. Shorter wavelengths provide better resolution. Blue light has the shortest wavelength among the given options, making it the best choice for achieving the best possible resolution.
Green light has a longer wavelength than blue light, so it would provide lower resolution compared to blue light.
Yellow light has a longer wavelength than green light, so it would provide even lower resolution than green light.
Red light has the longest wavelength among the options, and using it would result in the lowest resolution of all the choices.

questions.explanation: The resolution of a light microscope is inversely proportional to the wavelength of light used. Shorter wavelengths provide better resolution. Blue light has the shortest wavelength among the given options, making it the best choice for achieving the best possible resolution.

questions.text: According to the widely accepted "fluid mosaic model" cell membranes are semi-fluid, where lipids and integral proteins can diffuse randomly. In recent years, this model has been modified in several respects. In this regard, which of the following statements is incorrect?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Proteins can also undergo flip-flop movements in the lipid bilayer
options.explanation: The incorrect statement is A. In the fluid mosaic model, proteins are generally considered to be too large to undergo spontaneous flip-flop movements in the lipid bilayer. Instead, they can undergo lateral diffusion within the membrane.

options.text: Many proteins remain completely embedded within the lipid bilayer
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Many proteins are integral membrane proteins, meaning they are firmly embedded within the hydrophobic core of the lipid bilayer.

options.text: Proteins in cell membranes can travel within the lipid bilayer
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Proteins can move laterally within the membrane, allowing them to interact with different molecules and perform their functions.

options.text: Proteins can remain confined within certain domains of the membranes
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Proteins can be localized and restricted to specific regions or domains within the cell membrane, where they may perform specialized functions. These domains can be enriched in specific lipids and proteins, creating microenvironments with distinct properties.

questions.explanation: The incorrect statement is A. In the fluid mosaic model, proteins are generally considered to be too large to undergo spontaneous flip-flop movements in the lipid bilayer. Instead, they can undergo lateral diffusion within the membrane.An improved model of the structure of cell membrane was proposed by Singer and Nicolson (1972) widely accepted as fluid mosaic model. According to this, the quasi-fluid nature of lipid enables lateral movement (Flip-flop) of proteins within the overall bilayer. This ability to move within the membrane is measured as its fluidity.

questions.text: Centromere is required for:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Centromere is the point at which the two chromatids of a chromosome are held together. During movement of chromosomes, the spindle fibres (on which the chromatids move) are attached to the centromere. Crossing over involves physical exchange of genetic material between non­sister chromatids of homologous chromosomes. Cytoplasmic cleavage is the division of cytoplasm. Transcription is the process in which the genetic information of DNA is transferred to mRNA as the first step in protein synthesis.

Cytoplasmic cleavage is a process that occurs during cell division in which the cytoplasm of a cell is divided into two daughter cells. This process occurs in animal cells during mitosis and meiosis, and it is different from cell division in plant cells, which involves the formation of a cell plate. Cytoplasmic cleavage is important for the distribution of organelles and other cellular components between the daughter cells.

Crossing over is a process that occurs during meiosis, the type of cell division that produces gametes (sperm and eggs). During crossing over, homologous chromosomes exchange genetic material, resulting in new combinations of genes in the offspring. This process contributes to genetic diversity, as it creates new combinations of alleles that were not present in the parents. Crossing over occurs during prophase I of meiosis, after the chromosomes have replicated but before they separate into daughter cells.

Transcription is the process by which genetic information encoded in DNA is copied into RNA. This process is carried out by an enzyme called RNA polymerase, which reads the DNA sequence and synthesizes a complementary RNA molecule. Transcription is an important step in gene expression, as the RNA molecule produced can be used to direct the synthesis of proteins, which are the building blocks of cells. The RNA molecule produced during transcription can also have other functions, such as regulating gene expression or serving as a template for the synthesis of complementary DNA molecules.

questions.explanation: Centromere is the point at which the two chromatids of a chromosome are held together. During movement of chromosomes, the spindle fibres (on which the chromatids move) are attached to the centromere. Crossing over involves physical exchange of genetic material between non­sister chromatids of homologous chromosomes. Cytoplasmic cleavage is the division of cytoplasm. Transcription is the process in which the genetic information of DNA is transferred to mRNA as the first step in protein synthesis.

questions.text: Chemiosmotic theory of ATP synthesis in the chloroplasts and mitochondria is based on:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Chemiosmotic coupling hypothesis is the most widely accepted explanation for oxidative phosphorylation in mitochondria and photo-phos­phorylation in thylakoid membranes. Mitchell proposed the idea of chemiosmotic coupling. He suggested that a concentration gradient of protons is established across the mitochondrial membrane because there is an accumulation of hydrogen ions on one side of the mitochondrial membrane. The proton accumulation is necessary for energy transfer to the endergonic ADP phosphorylation process.

Mitchell proposed the idea of chemiosmotic coupling. The proton accumulation is necessary for energy transfer to the endergonic ADP phosphorylation process.

Mitchell proposed the idea of chemiosmotic coupling. He suggested that a concentration gradient of protons is established across the mitochondrial membrane because there is an accumulation of hydrogen ions on one side of the mitochondrial membrane. The proton accumulation is necessary for energy transfer to the endergonic ADP phosphorylation process.

questions.text: The main organelle involved in modification and outing of newly synthesized proteins to their destinations is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Chloroplasts are organelles found in plant and algal cells that are responsible for carrying out photosynthesis.
Mitochondria are organelles found in eukaryotic cells that are responsible for producing energy in the form of ATP.

Lysosomes are organelles found in animal cells that contain enzymes capable of breaking down various biomolecules, such as proteins, nucleic acids, carbohydrates, and lipids. Lysosomes are often referred to as the "garbage disposals" of the cell, as they are responsible for degrading and recycling unwanted cellular material.

The proteins formed on ribosomes pass into the ER lumen where they are modified. Then the modified proteins move on into the transitional area, where the ER buds off membranous sacs (the transport vesicles) that carry the proteins to the Golgi apparatus.

A short sequence of (12­-16 nucleotides in length) G-rich strands, as 3' overhangs, is another feature of telomeres.

options.explanation: Thylakoids are the flattened sac­-like membranous structures that are stacked on top of one another to form the grana of plant chloroplast. Chlorophyll and other photosynthetic pigments are situated in the thylakoid membranes, which are the site for the light­-dependent reactions of photosynthesis.

This technique is widely used for multiplying plants e.g., Chrysanthemum, Dioscorea floribunda, Coleus, Crotons, carnation plants, etc.

questions.text: Proteinaceous pigment which controls the activities concerned with light is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Phytochrome is a plant pigment that can detect the presence or absence of light and is involved in regulating many processes that are linked to day length (photoperiod), such as seed germination and initiation of flowering. It consists of a light­-detecting portion, called a chromophore, linked to a small protein and exists in two interconvertible forms with different physical properties, particularly in the ability to bind to membranes.

Chlorophyll is a green pigment found in the chloroplasts of plants, algae, and some bacteria. It is responsible for absorbing light energy during the process of photosynthesis, which is used to convert carbon dioxide and water into organic compounds such as glucose.

Anthocyanins are water-soluble pigments found in many plants that give them a red, purple, or blue color. They are responsible for the color of many fruits, vegetables, and flowers, such as blueberries, red apples, and purple grapes.

Carotenoids are a group of pigments found in many plants, algae, and some bacteria. They are responsible for the yellow, orange, and red colors of many fruits and vegetables, such as carrots, tomatoes, and bell peppers.

questions.text: Which cell organelle is concerned with the glycosylation of proteins?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Ribosomes are small, complex structures found in all living cells. They are responsible for protein synthesis, which is the process by which cells create new proteins. Ribosomes are composed of two subunits, one large and one small, that work together to read the genetic code in messenger RNA (mRNA) and assemble amino acids into new proteins. Ribosomes can be found in the cytoplasm of prokaryotic cells and in the cytoplasm and on the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) of eukaryotic cells. They are essential for life, as proteins are involved in many cellular processes, including metabolism, transport, and signaling.

Peroxisomes are small, membrane-bound organelles found in the cytoplasm of eukaryotic cells. They are involved in a variety of metabolic processes, including the breakdown of fatty acids and the detoxification of harmful substances such as alcohol. Peroxisomes contain enzymes that catalyze reactions involving hydrogen peroxide, which is a toxic byproduct of cellular metabolism. Peroxisomes are also involved in the synthesis of certain lipids and the metabolism of amino acids. They are found in most eukaryotic cells, including liver and kidney cells, and play an important role in maintaining cellular homeostasis.
Glycolysation of protein means linking of sugars to proteins which starts in the rough endoplasmic reticulum and is completed in the golgi complex.

Mitochondria are organelles found in most eukaryotic cells that are responsible for producing energy in the form of ATP (adenosine triphosphate). They are often referred to as the "powerhouses" of the cell. Mitochondria are enclosed by two membranes, an outer membrane and an inner membrane, which creates two distinct compartments: the intermembrane space and the mitochondrial matrix. The inner membrane is highly folded, forming structures called cristae, which increase the surface area available for ATP production. Mitochondria are capable of reproducing themselves by a process called fission, and they also contain their own DNA and ribosomes, which are involved in the synthesis of mitochondrial proteins. Mitochondria are essential for cellular respiration, which is the process by which cells convert glucose and other nutrients into ATP.

questions.text: Which of the following organelles has a single membrane?
questions.subject: Biology

Mitochondria are capable of reproducing themselves by a process called fission, and they also contain their own DNA and ribosomes, which are involved in the synthesis of mitochondrial proteins. Mitochondria are essential for cellular respiration, which is the process by which cells convert glucose and other nutrients into ATP.

The spherosomes are spherical bodies, about 0.5­-1um wide and enclosed by a single unit membrane. They contain granular contents rich in lipids but also have some proteins. They occur in most plant cells but are abundant in the endosperm cells of oil seeds. Spherosomes arise from the endoplasmic reticulum.

The nucleus is a membrane-bound organelle found in eukaryotic cells. It contains the cell's genetic material, including DNA and RNA, which is organized into structures called chromosomes. The nucleus is surrounded by a double membrane called the nuclear envelope, which contains nuclear pores that allow for the transport of molecules in and out of the nucleus. Within the nucleus, there is a nucleolus, which is involved in the synthesis of ribosomes. The nucleus plays a critical role in the regulation of gene expression, as it controls the transcription and processing of RNA molecules that are involved in protein synthesis. The size and shape of the nucleus can vary depending on the type of cell, and it is typically larger in cells that are actively dividing.

The cell wall is a rigid, protective layer that surrounds the cell membrane of many types of cells, including plant cells, fungi, and bacteria. It provides structural support and protection for the cell, helping to maintain its shape and prevent damage from external stresses. The cell wall of plant cells is composed mainly of cellulose, a complex carbohydrate that provides strength and rigidity. In addition to cellulose, plant cell walls may also contain other polysaccharides, proteins, and lignin. The cell wall of bacteria is composed of peptidoglycan, a unique molecule that is not found in eukaryotic cells. Fungal cell walls are composed of chitin, a complex carbohydrate that is also found in the exoskeletons of insects and other arthropods. The cell wall plays a critical role in many cellular processes, including cell division, growth, and communication.

The Golgi apparatus is a membrane-bound organelle found in eukaryotic cells. It is involved in the processing, sorting, and modification of proteins and lipids that are synthesized in the endoplasmic reticulum (ER).

The ribosomes provide space for the synthesis of proteins in the cell. Hence, they are known as the “protein factories” of the cell. The ribosomes bound to the membranes generally synthesize proteins for export as secretions by exocytosis, or for incorporation into membranes, or for inclusion into lysosomes. The free ribosomes generally produce enzymic proteins for use in the cell itself.

questions.text: Which of the following structures will not be common to a mitotic cell of a higher plant?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The centrioles occur in nearly all animal cells and in motile plant cells, such as zoospores of algae, sperm cells of ferns, and motile algae. They are absent in amoebae, prokaryotic cells, higher gymnosperms, and all angiosperms. An interphase (undividing) cell has a pair of centrioles (diplosome) usually near the nucleus. Spindle fibers, cell plates and centromeres are present in all plant cells.

Spindle fibers are protein structures that are involved in the separation of chromosomes during cell division. They are composed of microtubules, which are long, thin, protein fibers that help to maintain the cell's shape and are involved in many cellular processes. During cell division, spindle fibers attach to the centromeres of the chromosomes and pull them apart into two daughter cells.

Cell plates are structures that form during cell division in plant cells. The cell plate is important for the proper division of plant cells, as it allows for the formation of a new cell wall without disrupting the existing cell wall. The cell plate is a unique feature of plant cell division and is not present in animal cells.
A centromere is a specialized region of a chromosome that is involved in the attachment of the chromosome to the spindle fibers during cell division.

questions.explanation: The centrioles occur in nearly all animal cells and in motile plant cells, such as zoospores of algae, sperm cells of ferns, and motile algae. They are absent is amoebae, prokaryotic cells, higher gymnosperms, and all angiosperms. An interphase (undividing) cell has a pair of centrioles (diplosome) usually near the nucleus. They lie in a small mass of specialized, distinctly staining cytoplasm that lacks other cell organelles. The centrioles and the centrosphere are together referred to as centrosome. Before cell division, the centrioles duplicate so that a dividing cell has a pair of centrioles at each pole of the spindle. Spindle fibers, cell plates and centromeres are present in all plant cells.

questions.text: The mechanism of ATP formation, both in chloroplast and mitochondria, is explained by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Chemiosmotic coupling hypothesis is the most widely accepted explanation for oxidative phosphorylation in mitochondria and photo-phos­phorylation in thylakoid membranes. Mitchell proposed the idea of chemiosmotic coupling. He suggested that a concentration gradient of protons is established across the mitochondrial membrane because there is an accumulation of hydrogen ions on one side of the mitochondrial membrane. The proton accumulation is necessary for energy transfer to the endergonic ADP phosphorylation process.

Munch's hypothesis, also known as the mass flow model, is a theory that explains the movement of sugars and other nutrients in plants. According to this hypothesis, sugars are transported from the leaves where they are produced by photosynthesis to other parts of the plant through a pressure-driven mechanism. The sugars are loaded into the phloem sieve tubes in the leaves and then transported through the phloem to the rest of the plant. This movement of sugars is driven by a pressure gradient that is established between the source (the leaves) and the sink (the other parts of the plant). As sugars are loaded into the phloem in the leaves, they create a high concentration of solutes that causes water to move into the phloem by osmosis. This influx of water creates a high pressure in the phloem in the leaves, which drives the flow of sugars through the phloem to the rest of the plant. At the sink, sugars are unloaded from the phloem and used for growth and other metabolic processes. Munch's hypothesis is supported by experimental evidence, although it has been modified over the years to include other factors that influence the movement of sugars in plants.

The relay pump theory of Godlewski is a hypothesis that explains the movement of water in plants. According to this theory, water is transported in a series of relay pumps from the roots to the leaves. The relay pumps are composed of living cells in the xylem tissue, which are called "pump cells". These cells actively transport water from one cell to the next using energy from ATP. As water moves from one pump cell to the next, it creates a pressure gradient that drives the flow of water through the xylem tissue. The pressure gradient is highest at the roots and lowest at the leaves, which helps to move water from the roots to the leaves. The relay pump theory is supported by experimental evidence, although it has been modified over the years to include other factors that influence the movement of water in plants.

Cholodny-Went's model is a hypothesis that explains how plants respond to environmental stimuli such as light and gravity. According to this model, plants have specialized cells called statocytes that can sense the direction of gravity. When a plant is tilted, the statocytes detect the change in orientation and send a signal to the rest of the plant. This signal triggers a redistribution of the plant hormone auxin, which causes the plant to grow in a manner that compensates for the tilt. The redistribution of auxin is thought to occur through the action of an efflux carrier protein that pumps auxin out of the cells on the lower side of the stem and into the cells on the upper side of the stem. This creates a gradient of auxin that causes the plant to bend towards the light or gravity. The Cholodny-Went model is supported by experimental evidence, although it has been modified over the years to include other factors that influence plant growth and development.

questions.explanation: Chemiosmotic coupling hypothesis is the most widely accepted explanation for oxidative phosphorylation in mitochondria and photo-phos­phorylation in thylakoid membranes. Mitchell proposed the idea of chemiosmotic coupling. He suggested that a concentration gradient of protons is established across the mitochondrial membrane because there is an accumulation of hydrogen ions on one side of the mitochondrial membrane. The proton accumulation is necessary for energy transfer to the endergonic ADP phosphorylation process.

questions.explanation: Golgi apparatus is a stack of parallel, flattened, intercommunicating sacs or cisternae and many peripheral tubules and vesicles. The cisternae vary in number from 3 to 7 in most animal cells and from 10 to 20 in plant cells. The latter may contain a layer of parallel fibres, called intercisternal elements, that support the cisternae. The cisternae are free of ribosomes and have swollen ends. They look like the smooth endoplasmic reticulum.

The inner membrane of mitochondria is semi permeable and has many infoldings which are projected in the matrix, called cristae. It provides more surface area for cellular respiration and production of ATP in mitochondria.
Thylakoids, lamellae and grana are not part of mitochondria, they are present in chloroplast.

questions.explanation: The outer membrane of mitochondrion is smooth, freely permeable to most small molecules, contains fewer enzymes and is poor in proteins. It has porin proteins which form channels for the passage of molecules through it. It allows uptake of substrates and release of ATP. The inner membrane is semipermeable and regulates the passage of materials into and out of the mitochondrion. It is rich in enzymes and carrier proteins (permeases). It is usually produced into numerous infolds called cristae (singular crista). It bears minute regularly spaced lollipop­-shaped particles known as oxysomes. The rest of the inner membrane contains the electron carrier molecules of the electron transport chain.

questions.text: Besides giving out secretory vesicles, the Golgi apparatus is also concerned with the formation of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Golgi body is the series of flattened stacks which has two faces: cis and trans. The cis face of a Golgi stack is the end of the organelle where substances enter from the endoplasmic reticulum for processing, while the trans face is where they exit in the form of smaller detached vesicles. The trans face secretes lysosomes and secretory granules.
Formation of plastid, grana of chloroplasts and protein synthesis are not the function of golgi apparatus.
Formation of plastid, grana of chloroplasts and protein synthesis are not the function of golgi apparatus.
Protein synthesis is the function of ribosomes.

questions.explanation: The two poles of a Golgi apparatus are called cis face and trans face, which act respectively as the receiving and shipping departments. The vesicles lie near the ends and concave surface of the Golgi complex. They are pinched off from the tubules of the cisternae. They are of two types: smooth or secretory vesicles-which have a smooth surface and contain secretions of the cell-and coated vesicles, that have a rough surface and elaborate membrane proteins. They carry materials to or from the cisternae. The Golgi complex gives rise to primary lysosomes by budding from the trans face of cisternae.

questions.text: In plant cells, peroxisomes are associated with:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Peroxisomes are small, membrane enclosed organelles. It contains several oxidative enzymes for metabolic activity. Their main role is participation in photorespiration of C4 plants. It helps in oxidation of glycolate in photorespiration.
Phototropism is the growth of an organism in response to a light stimulus. It is controlled by the plant hormone, Auxin.

Photoperiodism is the response to changes in daylength that enables plants to adapt to seasonal changes in their environment. Photoreceptor protein in plants senses the seasonal change in length of night.
Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants and certain other organisms transform light energy into chemical energy.

questions.explanation: In plant cells, peroxisomes are associated with photorespiration. Peroxisomes are found only in C3 plants where a wasteful phenomenon of photorespiration occurs. The other organelles associated with this process are chloroplast and mitochondria. Peroxisomes are also reported in animal cells, i.e., liver and kidney cells. The peroxisome contains several enzymes such as amino transferse, glycolate oxidase, glyoxylate reductase, peroxidase and catalase etc.

questions.text: Membranous bag with hydrolytic enzymes which is used for controlling intracellular digestion of macromolecules is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Endoplasmic reticulum does not have hydrolytic enzymes and they don't digest any cellular parts.
Nucleosome is basic repeating unit of chromatin in nucleus. It also does not digest any cellular parts and doesn't have hydrolytic enzymes.

Lysosomes are membrane-bounded vesicles, rich in hydrolytic enzymes or digestive enzymes that are used for digestion of macromolecules. They contain about 40 types of hydrolytic enzymes including proteases, nucleases, glycosidases, lipases, phospholipases, etc.
Phagosomes digest the ingested particle with hydrolytic enzymes which are contained in lysozyme.

questions.explanation: A lysosome is a tiny sac bounded by a single unit membrane of lipoprotein. It contains a dense, finely granular fluid. The latter consists of glycoprotein hydrolytic (digestive) enzymes called acid hydrolases. These include proteases, lipases, nucleases, glycosidases, sulphatases, acid phosphatases, etc.

Higher plants and yeast are eukaryotic cells and have well defined organelles and nucleus.

questions.text: Cell recognition and adhesion occur due to biochemicals of cell membranes named:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Proteins are crucial components of cell membranes and are involved in various cellular functions, including cell recognition and adhesion. Many proteins in the cell membrane act as receptors and bind to specific molecules on other cells or extracellular matrix components, facilitating cell-cell interactions and adhesion. These proteins often have specific binding sites or domains that allow them to recognize and interact with other molecules, contributing to processes such as immune response, cell signaling, and tissue development.

Lipids are a class of biomolecules that include molecules such as fats, phospholipids, and cholesterol. While lipids play important roles in cell membrane structure and function, they are not directly involved in cell recognition and adhesion. Lipids primarily contribute to the fluidity and integrity of the cell membrane but do not have specific binding sites or recognition capabilities for other cells.

The cell membrane is composed of a lipid bilayer with embedded proteins. While both lipids and proteins contribute to the overall structure and function of the cell membrane, it is primarily the proteins that play a direct role in cell recognition and adhesion. Lipids, on the other hand, primarily contribute to the physical properties of the membrane, such as fluidity and barrier function.

Glycoproteins and glycolipids are molecules that consist of carbohydrates attached to proteins and lipids, respectively. These molecules are involved in cell recognition and adhesion processes. Carbohydrate chains present on the surface of glycoproteins and glycolipids can act as recognition sites, allowing cells to identify and interact with one another. These carbohydrate chains, often referred to as glycans, participate in cell-cell recognition events, immune response, and tissue development by facilitating specific interactions between cells.

questions.explanation: Cell recognition and adhesion occur due to biochemicals of cell membranes named glycoproteins and glycolipids. These are formed due to small carbohydrate molecules present on lipids and extrinsic proteins.

questions.text: In salivary glands, chromosomes/polytene chromosomes pairing is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Chromosomes/polytene chromosomes pairing takes place between homologous pairs of chromosomes.
Chromosomes/polytene chromosomes pairing takes place between homologous pairs of chromosomes.
Chromosomes/polytene chromosomes pairing takes place between homologous pairs of chromosomes.

Polytene chromosomes are large chromosomes found in the salivary glands of insects due to which they are also called salivary chromosomes. These chromosomes are found during the interphase stage of the cell division in the nuclei of the salivary gland cells of the larvae of Diptera (fruit fly or Drosophila melanogaster). These chromosomes undergo somatic pairing to form identical chromosomes which are joined along their length to one another. Somatic pairing is the pairing of homologous chromosomes during mitosis in a somatic cell (body cell other than sex cells).

questions.explanation: In salivary gland chromosomes/polytene chromosomes pairing is formed between homologous chromosomes. A characteristic feature of these chromosomes is that somatic pairing occurs in them and hence their number appears half of normal somatic cells.

questions.text: Which of the following is correct about cell theory in view of the current extent of our knowledge regarding cell structure?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Modified cell theory means that all living beings are composed of cells capable of reproducing.
options.explanation: While cell theory does involve the concept that all living beings are composed of cells, it does not specifically state that all cells are capable of reproducing. Some specialized cells, such as neurons, do not divide and reproduce like other cells. Therefore, the ability to reproduce is not a requirement for all cells in the context of cell theory.

options.text: Cell theory does not hold good because all living beings (e.g. viruses) do not have cellular organisation.
options.explanation: This statement is partially true in terms of viruses. Viruses are considered acellular entities because they lack cellular organization. However, cell theory is still applicable to the vast majority of living organisms that do possess cells. While viruses challenge the traditional definition of "cell," they are not considered exceptions to cell theory because they are not classified as living organisms.

options.text: Cell theory means that all living objects consists of cells whether or not they are capable of reproducing.
options.explanation: Cell theory does not require that all living objects consist of cells. It specifically states that all living organisms are composed of cells, emphasizing the importance of cells as the basic structural and functional units of life. Non-living objects, such as minerals or rocks, do not fall under the scope of cell theory.

questions.explanation: This statement is partly correct regarding viruses as an exception to cell theory due to their acellular nature. However, Paramecium, Rhizopus, and Vaucheria are all multicellular organisms and are not exceptions to cell theory. They consist of cells and adhere to the principles of cell theory.

questions.text: The binding of specific proteins on regulatory DNA sequences can be studied by means of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Ultra centrifugation is a technique used to separate and analyze biomolecules based on their density and size. While it is not commonly used to directly study protein-DNA interactions, it can be employed to isolate specific cellular components, such as organelles or protein complexes, that may be involved in protein-DNA interactions. By subjecting a sample to high centrifugal forces, molecules can be separated based on their sedimentation rates, allowing for the isolation and characterization of different cellular components.

Electron microscopy is a powerful imaging technique that uses a beam of electrons instead of light to visualize the structure of biological samples at high resolution. While it can provide detailed information about the morphology and organization of cellular components, including DNA and proteins, it is not typically used to directly study protein-DNA interactions. However, it can be employed to visualize protein-DNA complexes or structural changes induced by their interaction when combined with appropriate sample preparation techniques.

Light microscopy is a widely used technique that employs visible light to visualize and study biological samples. While it is not commonly used to directly investigate protein-DNA interactions, it can be utilized to observe the localization and dynamics of proteins within cells. Fluorescence microscopy, a type of light microscopy, is particularly valuable for visualizing fluorescently labeled proteins and DNA, allowing researchers to study their interactions and spatial distribution in living cells.

X-ray crystallography is a technique used to determine the three-dimensional structure of proteins and other macromolecules. In the context of studying protein-DNA interactions, X-ray crystallography can be employed to determine the atomic structure of a protein-DNA complex. By crystallizing the complex and analyzing the diffraction pattern of X-rays passing through the crystal, scientists can deduce the arrangement of atoms and the interactions between the protein and DNA.

questions.explanation: X­-rays crystallography is a technique which is used to study the binding of proteins on regulatory DNA sequences. In this technique, X-­rays pass through a crystal of a substance and form a diffraction pattern. With the help of this technique, the arrangement of atoms in the molecular structure of enzymes, proteins, DNA, etc. can be studied. Wilkins, Watson and Crick used this technique to determine the molecular configuration of double helix of DNA.
