Peroxisomes contain some oxidative enzymes like peroxidases, catalases and glycolic acid oxidases. They are abundant in liver cells where they are specifically involved in the formation and decomposition of hydrogen peroxide so they are named peroxisomes.

questions.explanation: Lysosomes are membrane bound, dense granular structures containing hydrolytic enzymes responsible mainly for intracellur and extracellular digestion.

questions.text: In a cross section, each centriole consists of nine triplets of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Microfilaments are the thinnest of the three types of cytoskeleton fibers. They are made up of actin filaments, and they are involved in a variety of cellular processes, including cell motility, cell division, and the formation of cell junctions.

Microvilli are short, finger-like projections that are found on the surface of some cells. They are made up of actin filaments, and they are covered in a layer of membrane. Microvilli increase the surface area of cells, which allows them to absorb more nutrients and other molecules from the environment.

In a cross section, each centriole appears as a ring of nine triplets of microtubules. The microtubules in the triplets are arranged in a clockwise or counterclockwise direction. The triplets are connected to each other by proteins, and they form a rigid structure that is essential for the function of the centriole.

Intermediate filaments are long, thin fibers that are found in the cytoplasm of cells. They are made up of a variety of different proteins, and they are arranged in bundles. Intermediate filaments provide strength and support to cells, and they also help to anchor organelles in place.

questions.explanation: In a cross section, each centriole appears as a ring of nine triplets of microtubules. questions.text: Which statement is correct about mitochondria and chloroplast?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Chloroplast and mitochondria cannot live independently
options.explanation: Chloroplasts and mitochondria are both semi-autonomous organelles, meaning that they have their own DNA and can produce some of their own proteins. However, they are not completely independent, as they still rely on the cell for some essential molecules, such as ATP and amino acids.

options.text: 70S ribosome is attached with the inner membrane of mitochondria and chloroplast
options.explanation: 70S ribosomes are attached to the inner membrane of mitochondria and chloroplasts because they are the type of ribosomes that are found in prokaryotes. Mitochondria and chloroplasts are thought to have originated from prokaryotic cells that were engulfed by eukaryotic cells, and they have retained some of their prokaryotic characteristics, including the use of 70S ribosomes.

questions.text: If 15 mm size object is observed under light microscope using 5X eyepieces and 10X objective, its magnified image size will be:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: If an object of 15 mm is observed under a light microscope using 5X eyepieces and 10X objective, the magnified image size will be 75 mm. The magnification of a microscope is the product of the magnification of the eyepiece and the magnification of the objective. In this case, the magnification of the eyepiece is 5X and the magnification of the objective is 10X, so the total magnification is 5X * 10X = 50X. The size of the image is equal to the size of the object multiplied by the magnification. In this case, the size of the object is 15 mm and the magnification is 50X, so the size of the image is 15 mm * 50X = 75 mm. Therefore, the magnified image size will be 75 mm.

questions.explanation: Magnification of a microscope is the combined effect of lenses. questions.text: In which type of cells, cell wall is not present:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Plant cells are an example of cells where the cell wall is present. The cell wall is a rigid outer layer that surrounds the cell membrane in plant cells, providing structural support and protection. It is mainly composed of cellulose, hemicellulose, and pectin. The presence of the cell wall is a characteristic feature that distinguishes plant cells from animal cells.

Bacterial cells are an example of cells where the cell wall is present. The cell wall in bacterial cells is located outside the cell membrane and is made up of peptidoglycan, a unique molecule not found in eukaryotic cells. The bacterial cell wall provides shape, rigidity, and protection to the cell. However, it's important to note that not all bacterial cells have the same cell wall composition. Some bacterial species may have different cell wall structures or lack a cell wall altogether.

Fungal cells are an example of cells where the cell wall is present. The fungal cell wall is different from the cell walls found in plant and bacterial cells. It is primarily composed of chitin, a tough and flexible polysaccharide. The fungal cell wall provides structural support and protection to the fungal cell and is an essential feature that distinguishes fungi from other organisms.

Liver cells, also known as hepatocytes, are a type of animal cell. Unlike plant, bacterial, and fungal cells, animal cells do not have a cell wall. Instead, animal cells are surrounded by a flexible and permeable cell membrane, also known as the plasma membrane. The cell membrane provides support and protection to the cell and regulates the movement of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.explanation: In animal cells, the outer most boundary is cell membrane, where as in plants it is cell wall.

questions.explanation: A portion of the conversion of cholesterol into vitamin D does indeed occur in the smooth endoplasmic reticulum (SER) of certain cells, particularly in the liver and skin. Here is an updated explanation of the synthesis of vitamin D from cholesterol, including the role of the smooth endoplasmic reticulum: Cholesterol, a precursor molecule for vitamin D synthesis, is obtained from dietary sources or synthesized in the body. In the skin, cholesterol is present in the sebaceous glands and epidermal cells. When the skin is exposed to UVB radiation from sunlight, a specific form of cholesterol called 7-dehydrocholesterol is converted into previtamin D3. Previtamin D3 is then transported to the liver, where it undergoes further modifications. The smooth endoplasmic reticulum (SER) of hepatocytes (liver cells) plays a role in this conversion process. In the liver, an enzyme called 25-hydroxylase, located in the smooth endoplasmic reticulum, adds a hydroxyl group to the previtamin D3 molecule, forming 25-hydroxyvitamin D3 [25(OH)D3], also known as calcidiol. This is the major circulating form of vitamin D in the bloodstream. From the liver, calcidiol is further metabolized in the kidneys. Another enzyme, located in the smooth endoplasmic reticulum of kidney cells, adds another hydroxyl group to calcidiol, resulting in the formation of the active hormonal form of vitamin D, known as calcitriol or 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D3 [1,25(OH)2D3]. Calcitriol is the biologically active form of vitamin D that exerts its functions in various tissues.

This option is the correct answer. Tay-Sach’s disease is because of the absence of an enzyme that is involved in the catabolism of lipids. Accumulation of lipids in brain cells leads to mental retardation and even death.

questions.explanation: Storage diseases are due to the absence of lysosomal enzymes. If a lysosomal enzyme responsible for lipid metabolism is absent, it leads to the accumulation of lipids in brain cells that causes mental retardation and death. Such storage disease is known as Tay-Sach's disease.

questions.text: Tay-Sach's disease occurs when cells build upto certain amaount of lipids in cell. Which organelle is responsible for the disease?

options.explanation: This is not the right choice. Accumulation of lipids in cells may occur due to not enough digestion of lipids. Digestion of such types of storage products is controlled by lysosomes. So, lysosomes may be responsible for the accumulation of these substances. Golgi apparatus is not responsible for such diseases.

This is the correct answer. Tay-Sach’s disease is because of the absence of an enzyme that is involved in the catabolism of lipids. Accumulation of lipids in brain cells leads to mental retardation and even death. Enzymes for catabolism of such substances are present in lysosomes. Hence, lysosomes are responsible for such types of diseases.

This option is incorrect. Mitochondria is not involved in metabolism of any type of substance. So, it is not responsible for accumulation of extra lipids Hence, storage diseases, like Tay-Sach's diseases, are not caused by mitochondria.

This option is incorrect. Though SER is responsible for the formation of lipids, it is not responsible for the accumulation of extra lipids. Accumulation of extra lipids may occur due to not enough digestion. For the digestion of such substances, enzymes are present in lysosomes. Hence, lysosomes are responsible for storage diseases like Tay-Sach's disease.

questions.explanation: Tay-Sach's disease is a genetic disorder that results in the destruction of nerve cells in the brain and spinal cord. It is due to the deficiency of lipids-digesting enzymes in the body. These enzymes are present in lysosomes. Hence, lysosomes are responsible for such types of storage diseases.

This option is incorrect. Glyoxysomes are present in plant seeds rich in lipids. They are the sites for breakdown of fatty acids to succinate. They are not involved in breakdown of old organelles.
This is the correct option. Lysosomes are involved in a process called autophagy (self-eating). During this process, some old, worn-out parts of the cell, such as old mitochondria, are digested.
This is an incorrect option. This organelle is specifically involved in the formation and decomposition of hydrogen peroxide in the cell.

questions.explanation: Lysosomes are membrane-bounded, dense granular structures containing hydrolytic enzymes responsible mainly for intracellular and extracellular digestion. They are also involved in the process called autophagy (self-eating). In this process, some old parts of the cell, such as old mitochondria, are engulfed and digested by lysosomes.

questions.text: Strictly speaking, autophagosomes are actually:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Golgi vesicles, containing digestive enzymes, budded off from the maturing face of the Golgi apparatus are called primary lysosomes, while autophagosomes are formed by the engulfment of old and unwanted parts of cells by primary lysosomes.

This option is incorrect. Tertiary lysosomes are the old secondary lysosomes that only contain waste material. On the other hand, autophagosomes are lysosomes that engulf and digest old, worn-out parts of cells.

This option is correct. Secondary lysosomes are formed by the fusion of primary lysosomes with food vacuole. Autophagosomes are also called secondary lysosomes because they are formed by the fusion of primary lysosomes with old, worn-out parts of cells that need to be digested.
This option is incorrect. There is no such structure or type of lysosomes in a typical cell.

questions.explanation: Secondary lysosomes are formed by the fusion of primary lysosomes with food vacuole. Autophagosomes are also called secondary lysosomes because they are formed by the fusion of old, worn-out parts of cells that need to be digested with primary lysosomes.

questions.text: Suicidal bags are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The only function of peroxisomes is the formation and decomposition of hydrogen peroxide in the cell. On the other hand, suicidal bags may refer to something that causes planned cell death.

This is the correct option. Lysosomes are involved in a process called autolysis which is a type of programmed cell death. Lysosomes contain digestive enzymes. When any cell needs to be disintegrated, lysosomes burst, and their digestive enzymes are released in the cytoplasm. These enzymes start digesting the parts of the cell, and the cell eventually dies. That's why, lysosomes are also called suicidal bags.

This option is incorrect. The function of mitochondria is energy synthesis for the cell, while suicidal bags refer to something that causes planned cell death. So, mitochondria is not the right choice.
This option is incorrect. Chloroplasts are involved in photosynthesis, i.e., synthesize food for the cell. They are not suicidal bags as they cause no harm to the cell.

questions.explanation: Lysosomes are involved in a process called autolysis which is a type of programmed cell death. In this process, lysosomes burst and their enzyme contents are quickly dispersed throughout the cytoplasm. In this way, the cell is disintegrated into fragments that are phagocytosed by other cells. Due to this function, lysosomes are also called suicidal bags.

questions.text: Which of the following eukaryotic organelle has symbiotic origin with bacteria?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Endosymbiotic hypothesis explains the symbiotic origin of some organelles with bacteria. Endoplasmic reticulum is not explained by this hypothesis.
This option is incorrect. The endosymbiotic hypothesis does not explain lysosomes having symbiotic origin with bacteria.

This option is the right option. According to the endosymbiotic hypothesis, ingestion of prokaryotes that resembled present-day cyanobacteria could have led to the endosymbiotic development of chloroplasts in plants. Hence, chloroplasts have a symbiotic origin with bacteria.
This option is incorrect. The endosymbiotic hypothesis does not explain the Golgi apparatus having any symbiotic origin with bacteria.

questions.explanation: The endosymbiotic hypothesis explains the symbiotic origin of chloroplast and mitochondria with bacteria. According to this hypothesis, ingestion of prokaryotes that resembled present-day cyanobacteria could have led to the endosymbiotic development of chloroplasts in plants.

questions.text: Chlorophyll molecule resembles with:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Carotenoids typically have a 40-carbon chain backbone composed of eight isoprenoid units. On the other hand, chlorophyll molecule has a head made of a porphyrin ring with magnesium as the central atom and a long side chain of carbon called phytol tail.

This option is incorrect. The globin chains of hemoglobin are actually polypeptide chains of amino acids. They don't have any resemblance with chlorophyll molecules as chlorophyll does not contain any amino acid chain.
This option is incorrect. Xanthophylls are derivatives of carotenoids, and as mentioned above, carotenoids don't have any resemblance with chlorophyll molecules.

This is the correct option. The haem part of hemoglobin is also a porphyrin ring as present in chlorophyll head. The only difference between chlorophyll and hemoglobin is that chlorophyll has Mg" while haem has Fe" as the central atom.

questions.explanation: Chlorophyll molecules resemble haem part of hemoglobin as both have a porphyrin ring in them. The only difference between chlorophyll and haemoglobin is that chlorophyll has Mg" while haem has Fe" as the central atom.

questions.text: Which of the following statement is incorrect?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This can't be the right choice because the given statement is true. CO2 fixation takes place in calvin cycle which occurs in stroma of chloroplast.
The given statement is also true, so this option is also incorrect. Chloroplasts have their own genetic material i.e., DNA. So they can replicate themselves.
This is the right option because the given statement is incorrect. Intergranum is colourless. It lacks chlorophyll. Hence, photosynthesis does not occur on it.
This is not the right option because the given statement is true. Chloroplasts are believed to be symbiotically originated from bacteria. So they have 70S ribsomes.

questions.explanation: Inter-granum is non-green part that connects two grana. The process of photosynthesis does not occur on inter-granum because of absence of chlorophyll.

questions.text: Which statement is correct about mitochondria and chloroplast?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Chloroplast and mitochondria cannot live independently.
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. According to the endosymbiotic hypothesis, both organelles are symbiotically originated from bacteria. It is found that both of them can survive independently outside the cell.

options.text: 70S ribosome is attached with the inner membrane of mitochondria and chloroplast.
options.explanation: This is the correct option. Mitochondria and chloroplasts both are considered as semi-autonomous organelles and are thought to be symbiotically originated from bacteria. They have their own circular DNA and 70S ribosomes just like bacteria.

options.text: Chloroplast and mitochondria are single membrane structure.
options.explanation: This is incorrect. Chloroplast and mitochondria both are double membrane-bounded organelles.

options.text: Number of mitochondria and chloroplast are same in all cells.
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The number of mitochondria and chloroplast varies in different types of cells. The number of mitochondria depends on the metabolic activity of the cell. Greater the metabolic activity, the greater is the number of mitochondria. Similarly, the number of chloroplasts depends upon the photosynthetic activity of the cell.

questions.explanation: Mitochondria and chloroplasts both are considered as semi-autonomous organelles and are thought to be symbiotically originated from bacteria. They have their own circular DNA and 70S ribosomes just like bacteria.

questions.text: Among followings which cellular organelle contains clrcular DNA similar to those found in bacteria?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Ribosomes are tiny granular structures found in the cell. They are composed of equal amount of RNA and proteins. They don't contain DNA.

This option is correct. According to the endosymbiotic hypothesis, chloroplasts are believed to be symbiotically originated from bacteria. They have their own circular DNA and 70S ribosomes, just like bacteria.
This option is incorrect. Lysosomes are derived from Golgi vesicles. They contain digestive enzymes for the digestion of phagocytosed food. They don't contain DNA.
This option is incorrect. Nucleas do have cell's genetic material, i.e. DNA, but it is not circular and is not similar to bacterial DNA.

questions.explanation: According to the endosymbiotic hypothesis, chloroplasts are believed to be symbiotically originated from bacteria. They have their own circular DNA and 70S ribosomes, just like bacteria.

questions.text: Each chloroplast contains_grana.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is not the right option. Each chloroplast conatin 40-60 grana.
This is the correct option. It is a fact. There are about 40-60 grana found in each chloroplast.
This option is incorrect. As mentioned above, chloroplast contain 40-60 grana.
This is incorrect option. As mentioned above, chloroplast contain 40-60 grana.

questions.explanation: It is a fact. There are about 40-60 grana found in each chloroplast.

questions.text: _ provides support for the individual plant cell and contributes to the turgidity of the leaves and younger parts of plants.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. Vacuoles are filled with cell sap and become turgid. In this way, they provide support for the individual plant cell and contribute to the turgidity of the leaves and younger parts of the plants.
This is an incorrect option. Though ER provides mechanical support to the cell, it does not contribute to the turgidity and turgor pressure.
This is an incorrect option. Providing support and turgidity to the cell is not the function of the Golgi apparatus.
This option is incorrect. Providing support and turgidity to the cell is not the function of the cytoskeleton.

questions.explanation: Vacuoles are filled with cell sap and become turgid. In this way, they provide support for the individual plant cell and contribute to the turgidity of the leaves and younger parts of the plants.

questions.text: What is the specific name given to biological membrane that surrounds the vacuoles?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is incorrect option. Plasma membrane surrounds the entire cell.
This is incorrect option. Plasmalemma is another name for plasma membrane that surrounds the entire cell.
This option is incorrect. Endomembrane is a membrane that is suspended inside the cell.
This option is incorrect. The tonoplast is the membrane that separates the vacuole from the cytoplasm of the cell.

questions.explanation: The tonoplast is the membrane enclosing the vacuole, also known as the vacuolar membrane. It maintains the turgor pressure of the cell. It controls the movement of molecules between the cytosol and sap.

questions.text: Presence of large central vacuole is the characteristic of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: They do not contain it.
They lack it.
They lack it.
Mature plant cells generally contain a large central vacuole develops by the joining of smaller vacuole.

questions.explanation: Mature plant cells generally contain a large central vacuole develops by the joining of smaller vacuole.

questions.text: Which of these is present in plant cell only?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Peroxisomes are found in both plant and animal cells. They are also found in protozoa, yeast, and many cell types of higher plants.
This option is incorrect. SER is found in all eukaryotic cells.
It is an incorrect option. Cytoskeleton is found in almost all types of cell.
This is the right option. Only plants have a large central vacuole and it is vital for their functioning. In other eukaryotes, it is not present in the center.

questions.explanation: Only plant cells have large central vacuole that provide support and turgidity to the plant cells. In other eukaryotes, it is not present in the center.

questions.text: The membrane separating the vacuole from cytoplasm is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is an incorrect option. Cristae are the infoldings present in the inner membrane of mitochondria.
This option is the correct. Tonoplast is the membrane that separates vacuole from the cytoplasm.
This option is incorrect. Cisternae is the membrane that separates the material present in the channels of endoplasmic reticulum from cytoplasmic contents.
This option is incorrect. The membrane surrounding vacuole is specifically called tonoplast.

questions.explanation: Tonoplast is the membrane that separates vacuole from the cytoplasm.

questions.text: Which of the following cell organelle can be viewed with the help of high power light microscope?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Endoplasmic reticulum is very small sized organelle. It can only be viewed with the help of electron microscope.
Due to large size of mitochondria, it can be visualized easily with the help of light microscope. Under light microscope, it appears to be rods or vesicles.
This option is incorrect. Ribosomes are very tiny granular structures present in the cell. They can only be viewed under electron microscope.
This is not the correct option. Golgi apparatus is also a very small-sized organelle. It can also be viewed only under the electron microscope.

questions.explanation: Due to large size of mitochondria as compared to endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi apparatus and ribosomes, it can be visualized easily with the help of light microscope. Under the light microscope, it appears to be rods or vesicles.

questions.text: It is the correct location of ATP synthase in mitochondria:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is not the correct option. ATP synthase is present in the inner membrane of mitochondria. The matrix does not contain ATP synthase.
It is not the correct option. Outer membrane is porous and freely permeable. It does not contain any enzyme.
This option is the correct. ATP synthase are present in the inner mitochondrial membrane at cristae.
This option is incorrect. The space present between the two membranes of mitochondria is called inter-membrane space. ATP synthase is not present in it.

questions.explanation: Fact: ATP synthase are present in the inner mitochondrial membrane at cristae.

questions.text: Which of the following structure is involved in oxidative phosphorylation?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct. Oxidative phosphorylation is associated with E.T.C. Electron transport chain (E.T.C) occurs in inner mitochondrial membrane which provides the site for pumping of protons across it. Oxidative phosphorylation is associated with E.T.C.
Thsi is not the correct option. Outer membrane of mitochondria is freely permeable and is not involved in oxidative phosphorylation.
This is not the correct option. Mitochondrial matrix is the site for Kreb's cycle. ETC does not occur in it.
It is not the correct option. Thylakoid membrane is the site for photo-phosphorylation during photosynthesis in plants. Oxidative phosphorylation does not occur on it.

questions.explanation: Electron transport chain (E.T.C) occurs in inner mitochondrial membrane which provides the proton gradient across it. Oxidative phosphorylation is associated with E.T.C.

questions.text: Krebs cycle occurs in the_of mitochondria.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is the right option. The Krebs cycle, also known as the citric acid cycle, is a series of chemical reactions that take place in the matrix of mitochondria. The mitochondrial matrix contains all enzymes and coenzymes that are required for Kreb's cycle.
This is an incorrect option. Stroma is the fluid present in the chloroplast of plant cells, while Kreb's cycle takes place in the matrix of mitochondria.

This option is incorrect. These particles are present in the inner mitochondrial membrane at cristae. These particles are actually ATP synthase enzymes that regulate the synthesis of ATP during the electron transport chain (ETC). Kreb's cycle does not take place on them.
This is an incorrect option. Cristae are the infoldings present in the inner membrane of mitochondria. They are the site for electron transport chain (ETC). Kreb's cycle does not occur on them.

questions.explanation: The Krebs cycle, also known as the citric acid cycle, is a series of chemical reactions that take place in the matrix of mitochondria. The mitochondrial matrix contains all enzymes and coenzymes that are required for Kreb's cycle.

questions.text: Which of the following cell type is more appropriate to study mitochondria?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: THey do not contain mitochondria.
They contain abundant number of mitochondria.
They contain abundant number of chloroplasts.
They do not contain mitochondria.

questions.explanation: Mitochondria are most abundant in muscle cells because of the strenuous metabolic activities, E.coli is a prokaryotic organism and lack mitochondria. RBC’s also lack mitochondria.

questions.text: In mitochondria, small knob-like structures called F1 particles are found in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Outer membrane of mitochondria is smooth and freely permeable. F1 particles are present in the infoldings present in inner mitochondrial membrane.

This option is the correct one. F1 particles are located on cristae that are the infoldings of inner mitochondrial membrane. F1 particle is actually ATP synthase, which plays role in synthesis of ATP through chemiosmosis.
This option is incorrect. Outer compartment is the space present between inner and outer mitochondrial membranes. It does not contain F1 particles.
This option is incorrect. Inner compratment of mitochondria contains the matrix. Matrix contains several enzymes and coenzymes required for metabolic processes, but it does not contain F1 particles.

questions.explanation: F1 particles are knob-like structures located on cristae, the infoldings present in the inner mitochondrial membrane. F1 particle is actually ATP synthase, which plays role in synthesis of ATP through chemiosmosis.

questions.text: Which organelle is bounded by two membranes?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Ribosomes are tiny granular structures found in the cell. They are not bounded by any membrane.
This option is incorrect. Lysosomes are single membrane-bounded vesicles containing digestive enzymes.

This option is the correct answer. Mitochondria are one of the organelles that are bound by two membranes. The outer membrane of mitochondria is smooth while the inner membrane has infoldings called cristae.
This option is incorrect. The nucleolus is a darkly stained body within the nucleus and is without any membranous boundary.

questions.explanation: Mitochondria are one of the organelles that are bound by two membranes. The outer membrane of mitochondria is smooth while the inner membrane has infoldings called cristae.

questions.text: The inner membrane of mitochondria forms extensive infoldings called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is the right option. The inner membrane of mitochondria has infoldings tha are specifically called cristae.
This option is incorrect. Lamella refers to a thin layer, membrane or plate of tissue.
This option is incorrect. Cisternae refers to the membrane that separates cytoplasmic contents from the materials present in the chennels of endoplasmic reticulum.
This option is incorrect. Matrix is the fluid present inside the inner mitochondrial compartment.

questions.explanation: The infoldings in the inner membrane of mitochondria are specifically called cristae.

questions.text: Functionally, mesosomes can be compared with:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. In bacteria, mesosomes is involved in respiration while the function of ribosomes is protein synthesis. Hence, they can't be functionally compared.

This option is incorrect. Polysome refers to many ribosomes attached to single messenger RNA that synthesize proteins. On the other hand, function of mesosomes is cellular respiration. Hence, they can't be compared functionally.

This is the correct option. Mesosomes are membrane infoldings in bacterial cells. They are the sites of cellular respiration. The electron transport chain occurs in mesosomes. In eukaryotic cells, cellular respiration takes place in mitochondria. Hence, both of them are functionally similar.
Ths option is also incorrect. Golgi bodies are involved in cell secretions. On the other hand, mesosomes are the site for cellular respiration in bacteria. They can't be functionally compared.

questions.explanation: Mesosomes are membrane infoldings in bacterial cells. They are the site for cellular respiration. The electron transport chain occurs in mesosomes. In eukaryotic cells. cellular respiration takes place in mitochondria. Hence, both of them are functionally alike.

questions.text: Organelle involved in aerobic respiration:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct. Mitochondria are the power house of a cell. They are the site for aerobic cellular respiration in eukaryotic cells.
This option is incorrect. Plastids are the site for photosynthesis and food storage in plant cells.
This option is incorrect. Lysosomes are involved in the digestion of phagocytosed food particles.
This option is incorrect. Ribosomes are involved in protein synthesis in the cell.

questions.explanation: Mitochondria are the power house of a cell. Aerobic cellular respiration takes place in mitochondria In eukaryotic cells.

questions.text: Inner membrane of mitochondria is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Cisternae is the membrane separating the channels of endoplasmic retilculum from cytoplasm.
This option is also incorrect. Lamella is a thin layer, membrane, or plate of tissue. The membrane of thylakoid sac is often termed as lamella.
This is the right option. Fact: The inner membrane of the mitochondria having infoldings is called cristae.
This option is incorrect. Tonoplast is the membrane surrounding the vacuole.

questions.explanation: Fact: The inner membrane of the mitochondria having infolds is called cristae.

questions.text: Which organelle is required for aerobic respiration?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Plastids are involved in photosynthesis and food storage in plant cells. Different types of plastids are involved in sugar synthesis, storage and pollination in plants.
This is the right option. Mitochondria is the power house of the cell. Aerobic cellular respiration occurs in mitochondria.

This option is incorrect. ER are involved in proteins and lipids synthesis. It also provide support to the cell and is involved in detoxification of harmful chemicals. Aerobic respiration does not occur in it.
This option is incorrect. Nucleus is the brain of cell. It contains the genetic material of the cell and controls all the cellular activites. It is not the site of cellular respiration.

questions.explanation: Mitochondria is the powerhouse of the cell. Aerobic cellular respiration occurs in mitochondria in eukaryotic cells.

questions.text: The finger like infoldings which are formed by inner membrane of mitochondria are called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Matrix is the fluid present in the inner compartment of mitochondria.
This is the right option. The finger like infoldings which are formed by inner membrane of mitochondria are called cristae.
This option is incorrect. Porins are proteins that create pores in the membrane. THey are present in the outer membrane of mitochondria and give it a sieve-like appearance.
This option is incorrect. Ribosomes are the tiny granular organelles found in the cell. They are the protein factories of cell.

questions.explanation: Fact: The finger like infoldings which are formed by inner membrane of mitochondria are called cristae.

questions.text: The structure in double-membrane bound organelle, primarily involved in ATP generation through cellular respiration is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is the right option. F1-particles are actually ATP synthase enzymes. It is responsible for the generation of ATP through chemiosmosis in mitochondria during cellular respiration.

This option is incorrect. A mitoplast is a mitochondrion that has been stripped of its outer membrane, leaving the inner membrane and matrix intact. It is used to study the physiology of mitochondria.

This option is inappropriate. Mitochondria is the name of the organelle where ATP generation takes place. F1-particles are the specific structures in the organelle that are responsible for ATP generation.
This option is incorrect. Ergastoplasm is another name for endoplasmic reticulum (ER) that is studded with ribosomes. It is invovled in the synthesis and secretion of proteins.

questions.explanation: F1-particles are actually ATP synthase enzymes. It is responsible for the generation of ATP through chemiosmosis in mitochondria during cellular respiration.

questions.text: F0-F1 particles is another name for:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. ATP synthetase is not any term used for F0-F1 particles.
This is the right option. The inner surface of cristae has granular structures called F0-F1 particles. These particles are actually ATP synthase enzymes.
This option is incorrect. Cytochrome complex is an electron carrier in eletron transport chain. It is not related to F0-F1 particles.
This can't be the right option because the correct answer is given in the above options.

questions.explanation: The inner surface of cristae has granular structures called F0-F1 particles. These particles are actually ATP synthase enzymes.

questions.text: Which of the following organelle has a continuous connection with nuclear membrane?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The Golgi apparatus does not have a continuous connection with the nuclear membrane. It is a freely floating organelle in the cytoplasm.
This is the right option. Fact: The rough endoplasmic reticulum(RER) is continuous with the nuclear membrane.
This option is incorrect. Lysosomes are freely floating digestive vesicles in the cytoplasm of the cell. They have no connection with nuclear membrane.
This option is incorrect. Smooth endoplasmic reticulum(SER) is not continuous with the nuclear membrane. It is connected to RER only.

questions.explanation: Fact: The rough endoplasmic reticulum(RER) is continuous with the nuclear membrane.

questions.text: It precisely describes the function of nucleoli:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Formation and breakdown of nuclear envelope
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The nuclear envelope is primarily formed during the process of nuclear reformation, which occurs after cell division. The breakdown of the nuclear envelope takes place during the early stages of cell division, specifically during prophase. Nucleoli are not directly involved in these processes.

This is the correct option. The nucleolus is the darkly stained area within the nucleus without any membranous boundary to separate it from the rest of the nuclear material. The nucleoli contain ribosomal subunits. These are said to be the factory of ribosomes while ribosomes are said to be the factory of protein synthesis.

This option is incorrect. The centromere is a region of DNA that plays a critical role in the separation of chromosomes during cell division. It is responsible for the attachment of spindle fibers, which help move the chromosomes. The formation of centromeres is not associated with nucleoli.

options.text: Organization of spindle during nuclear division
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The spindle apparatus, composed of microtubules, is responsible for the movement and separation of chromosomes during cell division. The organization of the spindle is controlled by various cellular structures, including centrosomes, but not nucleoli.

questions.explanation: The nucleolus is the darkly stained area within the nucleus without any membranous boundary to separate it from the rest of the nuclear material. It contains ribosomal subunits. These are said to be the factory of ribosomes while ribosomes are said to be the factory of protein synthesis.

questions.text: rRNA is actively synthesized in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Lysosomes are membrane-bound organelles involved in the digestion of phagocytosed food and recycling of cellular waste materials. They are not involved in the synthesis of rRNA.

This option is incorrect. The nucleoplasm refers to the contents of the nucleus, which include the nucleolus, chromatin, and other components. The nucleoplasm provides the environment for various nuclear processes, including transcription, but it is not involved in the synthesis of ribosomal RNA(rRNA).
This is the correct option. The rRNA is synthesized and stored in nucleolus. Central fibril area of nucleolus has large molecular weight ribosomal RNA and DNA.
This option is incorrect. The ribosomes are cellular structures composed of both rRNA and proteins. They are involved in protein synthesis but do not actively synthesize rRNA themselves.

questions.explanation: The rRNA is synthesized and stored in nucleolus. Central fibril area of nucicolus has large molecular weight ribosomal RNA and DNA.

questions.text: How many nuclear pores are present in a typical differentiated cell, such as RBCs?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. 30,000 pores per nucleus are present in an undifferentiated cell, such as ova. Differentiated cells have fewer nuclear pores than cells that are actively dividing. This is because they do not need to transport as many molecules between the nucleus and the cytoplasm.
This option is incorrect. Differentiated cells have fewer nuclear pores than cells that are actively dividing. They have about 3-4 pores per nucleas.

This option is incorrect. Differentiated cells do not need to transport as many molecules between the nucleus and the cytoplasm. About 30,000 pores per nucleas may be present in actively develoloping cells, such as ova.

This is the right option. The number of nuclear pores is more in undifferentiated cells as compared to differentiated cells. Since RBCs are fully differentiated cells, they have 3-4 nuclear pores, but the number of nuclear pores in egg cells is about 30,000.

questions.explanation: The number of nuclear pores is more in undifferentiated cells as compared to differentiated cells. Since RBCs are fully differentiated cells, they have 3-4 nuclear pores, but the number of nuclear pores in egg cells is about 30,000.

questions.text: Ribosomal RNA is synthesized in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is the right option. The function of nucleolus is the synthesis of ribosomes. It stores and synthesizes all the components needed for their synthesis. It is composed of two regions, the peripheral granular area composed of precursors of ribosomal subunits and the central fibril area consisting of large molecular weight ribosomal RNA and DNA.
This is an incorrect option. Golgi apparatus is not concerned with the synthesis of ribosomes or their components. So, it is not involved in the synthesis of ribosomal RNA.
This option is incorrect. Peroxisomes are specifically involved in the production and decomposition of hydrogen peroxide in the cells. It is not concerned with the synthesis of rRNA.
This option is incorrect. Nucleoplasm is the sap present inside the nucleas. It is involved in variuos processes like transcription etc. However, it is not concerned with the synthesis of rRNA.

questions.explanation: The function of nucleolus is the synthesis of ribosomes. It stores and synthesizes all the components needed for their synthesis. It is composed of two regions, the peripheral granular area composed of precursors of ribosomal subunits and the central fibril area consisting of large molecular weight ribosomal RNA and DNA.

questions.text: The structure present in a eukaryotic cells but absent in prokaryotic cells is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is the right choice. The term 'prokaryotes' is derived from the Greek word 'pro meaning before' and 'karyon meaning nucleus'. Prokaryotic cells are those cells that do not have a definite nucleus and membrane-bound organelles. On the other hand, a definite nucleus is present in eukaryotic cells.
This is an incorrect option. Ribosome is a non-membrane bounded organalle. Hence, it is present in both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells.

This option is incorrect. DNA is genetic material that contains all of the cell's information and is present in all types of cells. However, eukaryotic cells have their DNA enclosed in the nucleas while prokaryotic cells have their DNA freely dispersed in cytoplasm.
This option is incorrect. Cell surface membrane or plasma membrane is present both prokaryotic and eukaryotic. It surrounds the entire cell and is selectively permeable.

questions.explanation: The term 'prokaryotes' is derived from the Greek word 'pro meaning before' and 'karyon meaning nucleus'. Prokaryotic cells are those cells that do not have a definite nucleus and membrane-bound organelles. On the other hand, a definite nucleus is present in eukaryotic cells.

questions.text: It makes nucleoli during interphase:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The primary constriction refers to the narrowing of a chromosome at a specific region, usually where the centromere is located. It is not involved in nucleoli formation.
This is the correct answer. Secondary constriction is also called as nucleolar organizer. It gives rise to nucleoli during interphase.

This is an incorrect option. The centromere is a specific region of a chromosome that plays a vital role in the separation of sister chromatids during cell division. While the centromere is involved in various cellular processes, including chromosome segregation, it is not directly involved in nucleoli formation.

This option is incorrect. Telomeres are protective structures at the ends of chromosomes. They help maintain chromosomal stability and prevent degradation. Telomeres are not directly involved in nucleoli formation during interphase.

questions.explanation: Some chromosomes may have a point of union, other than primary constriction, along the length of chromatids. This is called secondary constriction, also known as nucleolar organizer. It gives rise to nucleoli during interphase.

questions.text: Which of the following statement is true?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Prokaryotic cells are bigger than eukaryotic cells.
options.explanation: This statement is wrong. Prokaryotic cells are not necessarily bigger than eukaryotic cells. Cell size can vary greatly within both prokaryotes and eukaryotes, and there are examples of small prokaryotic cells and large eukaryotic cells. For example ostrich's egg cell, which id eukaryote, is much larger than typical bacterial cell, which is prokaryote.

options.text: Prokaryotic cells evolved before eukaryotic cells.
options.explanation: This statement is true. Prokaryotic cells may have arisen more than 3.5 billion years ago while eukaryotes are thought to have first appeared about 1.5 billion years ago.

options.text: Eukaryotic cells do not have a nucleus, prokaryotic cells do.
options.explanation: This statement is wrong. Eukaryotic cells do have a nucleus, while prokaryotic cells do not. The nucleus is a membrane-bound organelle that houses the genetic material (DNA) in eukaryotic cells.

options.text: Eukaryotic cells are simple, prokaryotic cells are complex.
options.explanation: This statement is wrong. Both eukaryotic and prokaryotic cells can exhibit varying degrees of complexity, but in general, eukaryotes are more complex than prokaryotes.

questions.explanation: Prokaryotic cells may have arisen more than 3.5 billion years ago while eukaryotes are thought to have first appeared about 1.5 billion years ago.

questions.text: What is the main difference between prokaryotes and eukaryotes?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. In fact, prokaryotes are known for their ability to divide rapidly through binary fission, a process in which the parent cell divides into two daughter cells.

options.text: Prokaryotes do not have internal membranes.
options.explanation: This is the correct option. Besides nuclear envelope, the main difference between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cell is the absence of internal membranes or membrane bounded organelles.
This option is incorrect. They typically have a single, circular chromosome containing their genetic material. Some prokaryotes may also contain additional DNA in the form of plasmids.

This option is incorrect. Prokaryotes do have cytosol, which refers to the fluid component of the cell that surrounds the organelles. Cytosol contains various molecules, ions, and cellular structures necessary for cellular processes.

questions.explanation: Besides nuclear envelope, the main difference between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cell is the absence of membrane bounded organelles.

questions.text: Prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells generally have which of the following feature in common?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. A membrane-bounded nucleus is a characteristic specific to eukaryotic cells. Prokaryotic cells lack a distinct nucleus and have their DNA located in the nucleoid region.

options.text: A cell wall made up cellulose
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. A cell wall made up of cellulose is a feature specific to plant cells, which are a type of eukaryotic cell. Prokaryotic cells may have cell walls, but they are composed of different materials such as peptidoglycan in bacteria.
This is the right option. Both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells have ribosomes while membrane-bound nucleus, cellulosic cell wall and linear chromosomes are found only in eukaryotic cells.
This option is incorrect. A linear genome is a characteristic specific to eukaryotic cells. Prokaryotic cells typically have a circular genome, although exceptions exist.

questions.explanation: Both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells have ribosomes while membrane-bound nucleus, cellulosic cell wall, and linear chromosomes are found only in eukaryotic cells.

questions.text: The distinguishing feature of prokaryotic cell is its:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The chemical nature of DNA is not a distinguishing feature of prokaryotic cells. Both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells contain DNA, and the chemical composition of DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) is the same in both.

This option is incorrect. Ribosomes are present in both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells. Although there may be differences in the size and composition of ribosomes between the two cell types, their presence is not specific to prokaryotic cells.

This is the right option. The most distinguished feature of a prokaryotic cell is its cell wall. Chemically, it is composed of carbohydrates and short peptides. This complex molecule is called peptidoglycan or murein.

This option is incorrect. Cytoplasmic streaming movement is not a distinguishing feature of prokaryotic cells. Cytoplasmic streaming movement, also known as cytoplasmic flow or cyclosis, is a movement of cytoplasm observed in some eukaryotic cells, but it is not unique to prokaryotes.

questions.explanation: The most distinguished feature of a prokaryotic cell is its cell wall. Chemically, it is composed of carbohydrates and short peptides. This complex molecule is called peptidoglycan or murein.

questions.text: Peptidoglycan or murein is a special or distinctive feature of cell wall in:

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Algae can have diverse cell wall compositions, which may include a variety of components such as cellulose, pectin, silica, or other polysaccharides depending on the specific type of algae.
This is the correct answer. Bacteria is a prokaryotic cell. Prokaryotic cell wall is made up of peptidoglycan or murein which has glycan with short chain ef amino acids.

This option is incorrect. The cell walls of fungi contain a complex polysaccharide called chitin. Chitin is a nitrogen-containing polysaccharide that provides strength and rigidity to fungal cell walls.

This option is incorrect. Plant cell walls are primarily composed of cellulose, a polysaccharide made up of glucose units. Plant cell walls also contain other components like hemicellulose, pectin, lignin, and proteins, providing support and structural integrity to plant cells.

questions.explanation: Bacteria cell wall is made up of peptidoglycan or murein which has glycan with short chain ef amino acids. Fungi cell wall is made up of chitin, while plants have cellulose in their cell wall.

questions.text: Cell theory was formulated by Schleiden (1838) and Schwann (1839) which was later modified by:

Robert Whittaker was a notable biologist, but he is primarily known for his work in the field of taxonomy and the classification of organisms, particularly in proposing the Five Kingdom classification system in 1969. He had not modified the cell theory.
This option is incorrect. Madam Curie (Marie Curie) was a renowned physicist and chemist, known for her pioneering work on radioactivity, not specifically related to cell theory.
This is the correct answer. The cell theory, initially formulated by Matthias Schleiden in 1838 and Theodor Schwann in 1839, was later modified by Rudolf Virchow.

Antonie van Leeuwenhoek, often called the "Father of Microbiology," made significant contributions to the field of microscopy and was the first to observe microorganisms using his self-designed microscopes. questions.text: Keeping in view the fluid mosaic model for the structure of cell membrane, which one of the following statements is correct concerning the movement of lipids and proteins from one lipid monolayer to the other described as a flip-flop movement?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Both lipids and proteins can flip-flop
options.explanation: This statement is not correct because proteins cannot flip-flop. Lipids can flip-flop, but this process is very rare. Proteins, on the other hand, cannot flip-flop at all. This is because they are anchored to the membrane by either their amino acid side chains or by covalent bonds to the phospholipids.

options.text: Lipid can rarely flip-flop, proteins cannot
options.explanation: This option is correct. Lipids can flip-flop, which means that they can move from one leaflet of the membrane to the other. However, this process is very rare because it requires the lipid to pass through the hydrophobic core of the membrane, which is a high-energy barrier. Proteins, on the other hand, cannot flip-flop. This is because they are anchored to the membrane by either their amino acid side chains or by covalent bonds to the phospholipids.

options.text: Proteins can flip-flop, lipids can not
options.explanation: This statement is not correct because lipids can rarely flip-flop.

options.text: Neither lipids nor proteins can flip-flop
options.explanation: This staement is not correct because lipids can rarely flip-flop.

questions.explanation: Lipids can flip-flop, which means that they can move from one leaflet of the membrane to the other. However, this process is very rare because it requires the lipid to pass through the hydrophobic core of the membrane, which is a high-energy barrier. Proteins, on the other hand, cannot flip-flop. This is because they are anchored to the membrane by either their amino acid side chains or by covalent bonds to the phospholipids.

questions.text: Cells without nucleoli die because they do not possess:

options.text: Centrioles, and are unable to undergo cell division
options.explanation: Centrioles are involved in cell division, but they are not essential for cell survival.

options.text: Lysosomes, and are unable to destroy worn-out organelles
options.explanation: Lysosomes are involved in the breakdown of worn-out organelles, but they are also not essential for cell survival.

options.text: Mitochondria, and are unable to obtain energy
options.explanation: Mitochondria are responsible for generating energy for the cell, but they can also survive without ribosomes for a short period of time by importing proteins from other cells.

options.text: Ribosomes, and are unable to manufacture proteins
options.explanation: Cells without nucleoli die because they do not possess ribosomes, and are unable to manufacture proteins. Ribosomes are the protein factories of the cell. They are made up of RNA and protein, and they are responsible for translating the genetic information in DNA into proteins. Without ribosomes, cells are unable to synthesize proteins, which are essential for all cellular functions.

questions.text: Damage to one of the following immediately kills the cell whether it's prokaryotic or eukaryotic:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mitochondria are responsible for energy production in eukaryotic cells. Damage to mitochondria can impair cellular respiration and lead to energy depletion, which can eventually result in cell death. However, some cells, such as red blood cells, lack mitochondria and can survive without them.

The cell membrane, also known as the plasma membrane, is a crucial component of all cells. It forms a protective barrier that separates the interior of the cell from the external environment. If the cell membrane is severely damaged or disrupted, it can result in the loss of cell integrity and the inability to regulate essential cellular processes. This can lead to immediate cell death.

The cell wall is a rigid structure found in prokaryotic cells, fungi, and plants. While damage to the cell wall can compromise cell integrity and lead to various functional issues, it may not cause immediate cell death in all cases.

The Golgi apparatus is involved in the processing, modification, and sorting of proteins and lipids. Damage to the Golgi apparatus can disrupt cellular trafficking and secretion pathways, affecting cell function, but it may not immediately lead to cell death.

questions.explanation: The cell membrane, also known as the plasma membrane, is a crucial component of all cells. It forms a protective barrier that separates the interior of the cell from the external environment. If the cell membrane is severely damaged or disrupted, it can result in the loss of cell integrity and the inability to regulate essential cellular processes. This can lead to immediate cell death.

questions.text: Movement of Na+ across axon membrane via Na+-K+ pump is an example of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The Na+-K+ pump is an integral membrane protein that actively transports Na+ out of the cell and K+ into the cell, against their concentration gradients. In the case of the Na+-K+ pump, it uses the energy from ATP hydrolysis to pump three Na+ ions out of the cell for every two K+ ions it pumps into the cell.so this is an example of active transport in which forthe movement of ions or molecules across a cell membrane against their concentration gradient
Diffusion refers to the passive movement of ions or molecules down their concentration gradient, without the need for energy input.
Passive transport includes processes like simple diffusion, facilitated diffusion, and osmosis.

Osmosis specifically refers to the movement of water molecules across a selectively permeable membrane, driven by differences in solute concentration. It does not directly apply to the movement of Na+ ions via the Na+-K+ pump.

questions.text: Which one always passes through the nuclear membrane from nucleoplasm to the cytoplasm?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: They do not always pass through the nuclear membrane from the nucleoplasm to the cytoplasm. Proteins and enzymes can be synthesized both within the cytoplasm and the nucleus, and they can have diverse functions within these compartments.
DNA nucleotides are involved in DNA replication and transcription processes that occur within the nucleus and are not typically transported across the nuclear membrane.

They do not always pass through the nuclear membrane from the nucleoplasm to the cytoplasm. Proteins and enzymes can be synthesized both within the cytoplasm and the nucleus, and they can have diverse functions within these compartments.

RNA always passes through the nuclear membrane from the nucleoplasm to the cytoplasm. RNA molecules are synthesized within the nucleus through the process of transcription. After synthesis, RNA molecules need to be transported from the nucleus to the cytoplasm, where they can perform their functions in protein synthesis and other cellular processes.

questions.explanation: RNA always passes through the nuclear membrane from the nucleoplasm to the cytoplasm. RNA molecules are synthesized within the nucleus through the process of transcription. After synthesis, RNA molecules need to be transported from the nucleus to the cytoplasm, where they can perform their functions in protein synthesis and other cellular processes.

Lysosomes are small organelles that contain enzymes that break down worn-out organelles and foreign substances. Lysosomes are attached to actin filaments and myosin motor proteins, so they do move during cytoplasmic streaming.
Mitochondria are organelles that generate energy for the cell. Mitochondria are also attached to actin filaments and myosin motor proteins, so they do move during cytoplasmic streaming.

Glucose and Salts are ions that are essential for cell function. these are not organelles, but they are soluble in water and can diffuse through the cytoplasm. As a result, they can move along with the cytoplasm during cytoplasmic streaming.

questions.text: The outer and inner membranes of mitochondria are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The outer mitochondrial membrane acts as a protective barrier and separates the contents of the mitochondria from the cytoplasm of the cell. The inner mitochondrial membrane is highly impermeable and selectively permeable, regulating the passage of molecules in and out of the mitochondrial matrix, therefore they both are very different in their functions. The outer mitochondrial membrane is a relatively smooth and continuous membrane that surrounds the mitochondrion. The inner mitochondrial membrane is highly folded into numerous invaginations known as cristae, therefore they both are very different in their structure as well.

The outer mitochondrial membrane is a relatively smooth and continuous membrane that surrounds the mitochondrion. The inner mitochondrial membrane is highly folded into numerous invaginations known as cristae, therefore they both are very different in their structure, but their functions are definetly different.
They are structurally and functionally different.

The outer mitochondrial membrane acts as a protective barrier and separates the contents of the mitochondria from the cytoplasm of the cell. The inner mitochondrial membrane is highly impermeable and selectively permeable, regulating the passage of molecules in and out of the mitochondrial matrix, therefore they both are very different in their functions and they are structurally different .

questions.explanation: The outer mitochondrial membrane acts as a protective barrier and separates the contents of the mitochondria from the cytoplasm of the cell. It is relatively porous due to the presence of large channel-forming proteins called porins, which allow the passage of small ions,metabolites, and small proteins. The inner mitochondrial membrane is highly impermeable and selectively permeable, regulating the passage of molecules in and out of the mitochondrial matrix, therefore they both are very different in their functions. The outer mitochondrial membrane is a relatively smooth and continuous membrane that surrounds the mitochondrion. It contains a variety of proteins, including porins, which form channels through which small molecules can pass. The inner mitochondrial membrane is highly folded into numerous invaginations known as cristae. The presence of cristae greatly increases the surface area of the inner membrane, providing more space for proteins involved in oxidative phosphorylation, such as the electron transport chain complexes and ATP synthase, therefore they both are very different in their structure as well.

questions.text: Which substances can cross the plasma membrane more easily?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Ions are electrically charged particles, and some small ions, such as potassium (K+), sodium (Na+), chloride (Cl-), and calcium (Ca2+), can not move as readily as soluble lipids, but rather move switly across the plasma membrane through ion channels or transport proteins. These channels and transporters allow specific ions to move down their electrochemical gradients, regulating the electrical and chemical balance inside and outside the cell.

Lipids are an essential component of the plasma membrane, forming its phospholipid bilayer structure. Small lipid-soluble molecules, such as oxygen and carbon dioxide, can easily diffuse through the lipid bilayer and move across the plasma membrane.

Proteins are large molecules, and their movement across the plasma membrane is typically more restricted. Some small proteins might be able to pass through specific transporters or channels, but in general, larger proteins do not freely cross the membrane without active transport mechanisms.

Starch is a complex carbohydrate composed of many glucose units and is too large and hydrophilic to pass through the hydrophobic core of the plasma membrane. Carbohydrates,including starch, generally require specific transporters or active transport processes to cross the membrane.

questions.explanation: Soluble lipids have the highest likelihood of crossing the plasma membrane more easily due to them being easily diffusable . Small proteins, and small molecules like oxygen and carbon dioxide can also pass through the lipid bilayer to some extent. Large molecules such as starch require more specialized mechanisms for transport across the plasma membrane.

A polysome, also known as a polyribosome, is a complex of multiple ribosomes simultaneously translating a single mRNA molecule in eukaryotic cells. According to this statement, there should be only one mRNA and many ribsomes, therefore it'll be wrong.

A polysome, also known as a polyribosome, is a complex of multiple ribosomes simultaneously translating a single mRNA molecule in eukaryotic cells. The ribosomes work in a co-ordinated manner to produce multiple copies of the same protein, which allows for efficient and rapid protein synthesis.

questions.explanation: A polysome, also known as a polyribosome, is a complex of multiple ribosomes simultaneously translating a single mRNA molecule in eukaryotic cells. The ribosomes work in a co-ordinated manner to produce multiple copies of the same protein, which allows for efficient and rapid protein synthesis. Polysomes are often found where a high demand for proteins exists. The polysome assembly is facilitated by the fact that mRNA molecules in eukaryotic cells are typically much longer than prokaryotic mRNA, and they remain associated with multiple ribosomes as they are being translated.

questions.text: Which one is a self-replicating organelle?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Ribosome assists in creating protein and polypeptides. It does not have a nucleus or any genetic material of it own, therefore it can not reproduce.
Centrioles play an important role in cell division, however it does not replicate on their own.

Lysosomes consist so digestive juices and help break down complex substances into smaller and finer substances. It works on cellular level, however it does not have a nucleus of its own, therefore it can not reproduce.
Mitochondria has a nucleus and it can therefore reproduce on its own.

questions.explanation: Mitochondria are capable of self-replication through a process called mitochondrial fission. Mitochondria are double-membraned organelles found in eukaryotic cells, and they are often referred to as the "powerhouses of the cell" because they generate most of the cell's supply of adenosine triphosphate (ATP), which is used as a source of chemical energy. Mitochondrialfission is the process by which a single mitochondrion divides into two daughter mitochondria. This division is essential for maintaining the population and distribution of mitochondria within the cell. It allows mitochondria to multiply in response to the cell's energy needs, adapt to changes in cellular conditions, and repair damaged mitochondria.

questions.text: The absence of an enzyme that is involved in the catabolism of lipids results in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In Tay-Sachs disease, lipid storage refers to the abnormal accumulation of a specific lipid within the nerve cells of the brain and spinal cord. It is a complex molecule found in the brain.
Glycogenosis type II is a gycogen storage disease and not a fat storage disease.
Glycogenosis type I is a gycogen storage disease and not a fat storage disease.
Phenylketonuria is a rare and serious disease in which certain amino acid do not digest.

questions.explanation: In Tay-Sachs disease, lipid storage refers to the abnormal accumulation of a specific lipid within the nerve cells of the brain andspinal cord. The accumulation of the lipid occurs due to a deficiency or complete absence of a specific enzyme called hexosaminidase A (Hex-A). This enzyme is responsible for breaking down the complex substance into simpler components that can be recycled or eliminated from the cells. In individuals with Tay-Sachs disease, mutations in the gene lead to the defective production of the enzyme. As a result, the lipid cannot be broken down properly and starts to accumulate within the lysosomes, which are cellular structures responsible for breaking down various substances.

questions.text: The lysosomes which eat parts of their cells or cellular components during cellular starvation are called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Primary lysosomes are newly formed lysosomes containing a variety of hydrolytic enzymes, also known as digestive enzymes. These enzymes are synthesized in the endoplasmicreticulum and then transported to the Golgi apparatus, where they are packaged into vesicles, which subsequently mature into primary lysosomes. The primary function of primary lysosomes is to store these digestive enzymes.

Secondary lysosomes form when primary lysosomes fuse with endocytic vesicles or phagosomes. Endocytic vesicles are vesicles formed by endocytosis, which is the process of bringing extracellular material into the cell. Phagosomes, on the other hand, are vesicles formed when the cell engulfs large particles, such as bacteria or debris. When primary lysosomes fuse with these vesicles, they become secondary lysosomes.

Tertiary lysosomes, also known as residual bodies or residual lysosomes, are lysosomes that contain indigestible or non-recyclable material. After the secondary lysosomes have completed digestion, there may be certain materials or substances that cannot be broken down further or cannot be effectively recycled by the cell. These undigested remnants accumulate within the lysosome, and it becomes a tertiary lysosome.

The lysosomes that eat parts of their cells or cellular components during cellular starvation are called autophagosomes or autophagic vacuoles. The process of lysosomes engulfing and breaking down their own cellular components, or damaged organelles, through autophagy is called autophagy or autophagic degradation.

questions.explanation: Autophagy is a catabolic process in eukaryotic cells that delivers cytoplasmic components and organelles to the lysosomes for digestion. Autophagosomes are lysosomes are specialized organelles that break up macromolecules, allowing the cell to reuse the materials.

Cisternae are present in the Golgi apparatus. The Golgi apparatus, also known as the Golgi complex or Golgi body, is a cellular organelle found in eukaryotic cells. It is involved in the modification, sorting, and packaging of proteins and lipids synthesized in the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) for transport to various destinations within the cell or for secretion outside the cell.

questions.explanation: Grana are stacks of thylakoids where the light-dependent reactions take place, thylakoids are individual membrane-bound structures containing the photosynthetic pigments, and the stroma is the gel-like fluid surrounding the grana where the light-independent reactions occur. The arrangement of thylakoids in grana increases the surface area available for light absorption, optimizing the efficiency of photosynthesis.

The function of the nucleolus is to make ribosomes. The nucleolus is a distinct subcompartment within the cell nucleus, and it plays a vital role in ribosome biogenesis, which is the process of producing ribosomes, the cellular machinery responsible for protein synthesis.

questions.explanation: The function of the nucleolus is to make ribosomes. The nucleolus is a distinct subcompartment within the cell nucleus, and it plays a vital role in ribosome biogenesis, which is the process of producing ribosomes, the cellular machinery responsible for protein synthesis. The nucleolus is responsible for making ribosomes by transcribing, processing, and assembling the components of ribosomes before exporting them to the cytoplasm, where they perform their crucial role in protein synthesis. Ribosomes are essential cellular structures involved in protein synthesis. They read the genetic information encoded in messenger RNA (mRNA) and use this information to assemble amino acids into polypeptide chains, which eventually fold into functional proteins.

Fermentation is a metabolic process that does not occur in the mitochondria. Instead, it takes place in the cytoplasm of cells. options.explanation: An interconnecting network of cisternae (elongated closed sacs) which is generally extended from nuclear membrane to the plasma membrane throughout the cytoplasm of all eukaryotic cells is called endoplasmic reticulum (ER).

Microtubules are part of the cytoskeleton, a structural network within the cell's cytoplasm. The roles of the microtubule cytoskeleton include mechanical support, organization of the cytoplasm, transport, motility and chromosome segregation.

Endoplasmic reticulum being a network present all around the nucleus upto cell membrane acts like spokes of a wheel to maintain the shape of the cell.

questions.explanation: A Golgi body is flattened membrane bound sac whereas a Golgi apparatus is a stack of Golgi bodies, whereas as Golgi complex is a Golgi apparatus along with transport vesicles of endoplasmic reticulum and Golgi vesicles

questions.text: Inner concave surface of Golgi apparatus is:

options.explanation: Inner concave face is called maturation face.
It is the outer or forming face which is convex.
Outer or forming face is convex.

questions.explanation: It is also called trans face being away from the endoplasmic reticulum. From this face transport vesicles of Golgi bodies are pinched off having finished products inside them. That is why it is called maturation face

questions.text: The direction of flow of protein products synthesized on ribosomes is as under:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Ribosomes —› RER —› Transport Vesicles of ER —› Golgi apparatus —› Golgi vesicles —› Plasma membrane
options.explanation: This is the true sequence where protein produsts move from ribosomes to RER , then to transport vesicles of ER to Golgi apparatus and its vesicles and finally fuse with plasma membrane.

options.text: Ribosomes —› SER —› Transport vesicles of ER —› Golgi apparatus —› Golgi vesicles —› Plasma membrane
options.explanation: SER is not involved in protein synthesis.

options.text: Ribosomes —› RER —› Golgi vesicles—› Golgi apparatus —› Plasma membrane
options.explanation: Golgi apparatus comes before vesicles.

options.text: Ribosomes —› RER —› Golgi apparatus —› Golgi vesicles —› Plasma membrane
options.explanation: Transport vesicles are missing.

questions.explanation: Raw proteins are synthesized by ribosomes on RER and then dumped into cisternae of RER. From RER these raw proteins are pinched off in transport vesicles of RER. These transportvesicles of RER are fused together at forming face of Golgi apparatus to form golgi bodies. During the movement of these golgi bodies from forming face to maturation face these raw proteins are converted into finished products (conjugated molecules). Then these are packed in golgi vesicles which are pinched off from maturation face. These vesicles move to the cell membrane for exocytosis or secretion as primary lysosomes.

questions.explanation: Blebs from tips of SER are actually transport vesicles of endoplasmic reticulum which coalesce together to give rise to a golgi body at the forming face of golgi apparatus

questions.text: Which one of the following is a congenital disease?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: It is not congenital.Uremia, a clinical condition associated with worsening renal function, is characterized by fluid, electrolyte, hormone imbalances, and metabolic abnormalities.

It is congenital. It is a lysosomal disorder which is inherited by birth as a genetic deficiency. A glycogen storage disease (GSD, also glycogenosis and dextrinosis) is a metabolic disorder caused by a deficiency of an enzyme or transport protein affecting glycogen synthesis, glycogen breakdown, or glucose breakdown, typically in muscles and/or liver cells.
It is not congenital. Myocardial infarction (MI), colloquially known as "heart attack," is caused by decreased or complete cessation of blood flow to a portion of the myocardium.
It is not congenital. Also called ischemic stroke, a cerebral infarction occurs as a result of disrupted blood flow to the brain due to problems with the blood vessels that supply it.

questions.explanation: It is a lysosomal disorder which is inherited by birth as a genetic deficiency.

questions.text: Which one of the following is not true about Tay sach's disease?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: It is a mental disorder. Affected children appear to develop without a problem until about 6 months of age and then begin to show neurological symptoms, including: Slowing of development. Progressive loss of mental ability.

It is a congenital disorder. Tay-Sachs disease is a genetic disorder that is passed from parents to their children. It occurs when a child inherits a flaw (mutation) in the HEXA gene from both parents. The genetic change that causes Tay-Sachs disease results in a deficiency of the enzyme beta-hexosaminidase A.
It is a lysosomal disorder in which lysosomes do not contain the enzyme to digest lipids.

It is not a cardiovascular disorder.CVDs are a group of disorders of the heart and blood vessels and include coronary heart disease, cerebrovascular disease, rheumatic heart disease and other conditions.

questions.explanation: Tay sach’s diseases is a congenital disorder due to deficiency of a particular lysosome (enzyme) associated with catabolism of lipids. As a result, lipids accumulate in brain and a mentaldisorder appears which may lead to death.

questions.text: Tay sach's disease is due to deficiency of an enzyme associated with:
questions.subject: Biology
